opts: End-to-end communication, segmentation, flow control, and error recovery between hosts (TCP/UDP operate here)
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Layer 4 (Transport) provides end-to-end communication between applications. TCP provides reliable, ordered delivery with error recovery. UDP provides fast, unreliable delivery. Ports are a Transport layer concept. Handles segmentation and reassembly.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Transport Layer (L4) = End-to-end comms, TCP/UDP, ports, segmentation, flow control

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Git?
opts: A distributed version control system for tracking changes in source code during software development
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Git is a distributed VCS created by Linus Torvalds in 2005. Every developer has a full copy of the repository (distributed). It tracks changes, supports branching/merging, and enables collaboration. GitHub/GitLab/Bitbucket host Git repositories.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Git = Distributed VCS — every developer has full repo copy; tracks all code changes

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the difference between 'git merge' and 'git rebase'?
opts: git merge combines branches preserving commit history; git rebase moves/replays commits onto another branch creating a linear history
git merge is for local changes; git rebase is for remote changes
git merge is faster; git rebase is safer
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: 'git merge' creates a merge commit combining two branches — preserves full history including branch topology. 'git rebase' replays commits from one branch onto another, creating a cleaner linear history but rewrites commit hashes. Never rebase public/shared branches.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: merge = preserve history + merge commit; rebase = linear history (rewrites commits — don't rebase public branches)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Git Pull Request (PR)?
opts: A request to pull code from a remote repository
A proposal to merge code changes from one branch into another, enabling code review before integration
A Git command to download changes
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: A Pull Request (GitHub/Bitbucket) or Merge Request (GitLab) is a code review mechanism. The developer proposes merging their branch, teammates review the changes, leave comments, request modifications, and approve before merging to the main branch.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Pull Request = Code review gate before merging branch — discuss, review, approve, then merge

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is 'git stash' used for?
opts: Temporarily saves (stashes) uncommitted work-in-progress changes so you can switch context, then restores them later
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: 'git stash' saves your uncommitted changes (both staged and unstaged) onto a temporary stack and reverts your working directory to HEAD. Use 'git stash pop' to restore. Useful for switching branches without committing incomplete work.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: git stash = Temp save uncommitted changes — switch context, then 'git stash pop' to restore

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Git tag?
opts: A label applied to commits marking specific points in history, typically used to mark release versions
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Git tags mark specific commits as important — typically used for release versions (v1.0.0, v2.1.3). Unlike branches, tags don't move as new commits are added. Annotated tags store metadata (message, author); lightweight tags are just pointers.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Git tag = Permanent pointer to a specific commit — used to mark release versions (v1.0.0)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What does 'git cherry-pick' do?
opts: Applies a specific commit from one branch onto the current branch without merging the entire branch
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: 'git cherry-pick <commit-hash>' applies the changes introduced by a specific commit onto the current branch. Useful for applying a specific bug fix from a feature branch to the main branch without merging all feature changes.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: git cherry-pick = Apply a specific commit to current branch — without merging the whole branch

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the Gitflow branching strategy?
opts: Using only one branch for all development
A branching model using main, develop, feature, release, and hotfix branches for structured release management
A strategy for managing Git repositories across teams
A Git configuration for distributed teams
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Gitflow uses: main (production), develop (integration), feature/* (new features), release/* (release preparation), hotfix/* (production fixes). Provides clear separation of concerns but can be complex. Alternatives: GitHub Flow, trunk-based development.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Gitflow = main + develop + feature + release + hotfix branches — structured but complex

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What does the 'chmod 755' command do in Linux?
opts: Sets file permissions to rwxr-xr-x — owner can read/write/execute, group and others can only read and execute
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: chmod 755 sets: owner (7=rwx), group (5=r-x), others (5=r-x). Octal notation: 4=read, 2=write, 1=execute. Common for executables and web server directories. 644 is typical for files (rw-r--r--).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: chmod 755 = rwxr-xr-x (owner:rwx, group:r-x, others:r-x) — 4+2+1=7, 4+1=5

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Linux process and how do you list all running processes?
opts: A Linux user account; use 'ls -la' to list
An executing program instance; use 'ps aux' or 'top'/'htop' to list all running processes
A system file; use 'cat /proc' to list
A network connection; use 'netstat' to list
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: A process is a running instance of a program with its own PID, memory space, and resources. 'ps aux' shows all processes (a=all, u=user format, x=without terminal). 'top' and 'htop' provide interactive real-time process views.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Process = Running program instance; 'ps aux' = list all processes; 'top'/'htop' = real-time

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What does 'grep -r "error" /var/log/' do?
opts: Recursively searches for the string 'error' in all files under /var/log/ and prints matching lines
Counts the number of log files
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: 'grep' searches for patterns in files. -r flag makes it recursive (searches all subdirectories). This command finds all occurrences of 'error' (case-sensitive) in all log files under /var/log/. Use -i for case-insensitive search.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: grep -r 'pattern' /path = Recursively search for pattern in all files under path

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the purpose of the /etc/passwd file in Linux?
opts: Stores all user passwords in plaintext
Stores user account information (username, UID, GID, home directory, shell) — passwords are stored in /etc/shadow
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: /etc/passwd contains one entry per user with: username, x (password placeholder), UID, GID, description, home directory, and login shell. Actual hashed passwords are in /etc/shadow (readable only by root) for security.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: /etc/passwd = User account info (UID/GID/home/shell); actual password hashes in /etc/shadow

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the difference between a soft link (symlink) and a hard link in Linux?
opts: A soft link is a pointer to a file path (breaks if source is deleted); a hard link is a direct pointer to the inode (survives source deletion)
A soft link is faster; a hard link is more secure
Hard links work across filesystems; soft links do not
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Soft links (ln -s) point to a file path — if the original is deleted, the symlink breaks. Hard links (ln) point to the same inode as the original — both filenames reference the same data. Hard links cannot cross filesystems or link to directories.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Symlink = path pointer (breaks if source deleted); Hard link = same inode (survives deletion)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the difference between TCP and UDP?
opts: TCP is faster; UDP is more reliable
TCP is connection-oriented with guaranteed delivery, ordering, and error recovery; UDP is connectionless with no delivery guarantee — faster but unreliable
TCP is for HTTP; UDP is for HTTPS
TCP uses more ports; UDP uses fewer ports
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: TCP (Transmission Control Protocol): connection-oriented, uses 3-way handshake, guarantees delivery with acknowledgments, ordered delivery, and retransmission. Slower but reliable. UDP: no connection, no ACKs, faster, used for streaming, gaming, DNS.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: TCP = reliable, ordered, connection-oriented (HTTP/SSH); UDP = fast, unreliable (DNS/streaming)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is DNS and how does it work?
opts: A hierarchical naming system that translates human-readable domain names (google.com) into IP addresses through recursive and authoritative name server queries
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: DNS resolution: browser checks local cache → OS cache → Recursive resolver → Root nameserver → TLD nameserver → Authoritative nameserver → IP returned. The recursive resolver does the heavy lifting, caching results for the TTL period.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: DNS = Domain → IP translation; Recursive resolver queries Root → TLD → Authoritative NS

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a subnet mask and what does /24 mean?
opts: A firewall rule type; /24 means 24 firewall rules
A mask that defines which portion of an IP address is the network vs host; /24 means first 24 bits are network, allowing 254 usable hosts
A routing metric; /24 means 24 hops maximum
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: /24 (255.255.255.0) means the first 24 bits identify the network, leaving 8 bits for hosts (256 - 2 = 254 usable hosts). /16 = 65,534 hosts, /28 = 14 hosts. CIDR notation replaces the older class-based networking.

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is NAT (Network Address Translation)?
opts: A technique that translates private IP addresses to a public IP for internet communication — conserves public IPs and provides security
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: NAT translates private IP addresses (192.168.x.x, 10.x.x.x) to a public IP for internet communication. PAT (Port Address Translation) allows multiple devices to share one public IP using different ports. Common in home routers and cloud NAT gateways.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: NAT = Private IP ↔ Public IP translation — PAT allows many devices to share one public IP

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the purpose of a VPN?
opts: To create an encrypted tunnel over the public internet to securely connect remote users or sites to a private network
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: A VPN creates an encrypted tunnel between a client and a private network over the public internet. Common uses: remote workers accessing corporate resources, site-to-site connections between offices, and privacy/security on public WiFi.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: VPN = Encrypted tunnel over internet — secure remote access to private network resources

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is SAST (Static Application Security Testing)?
opts: Runtime security scanning of deployed apps
Analysis of source code, bytecode, or binaries without executing the application to find vulnerabilities early in development
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: SAST analyzes source code or compiled code without running it, detecting vulnerabilities like SQL injection, XSS, buffer overflows, and insecure functions. Integrates into CI pipelines. Tools: SonarQube, Checkmarx, Semgrep, CodeQL.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: SAST = Static code analysis — find vulnerabilities in source code without running it (CI integration)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is DAST (Dynamic Application Security Testing)?
opts: Security testing that attacks a running application from the outside to find exploitable vulnerabilities in real runtime behavior
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: DAST tests running applications by simulating external attacks (like a hacker would). It sends malicious inputs (SQL injection, XSS payloads) and analyzes responses. Tools: OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite. Finds runtime vulnerabilities SAST misses.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: DAST = Attack running app from outside — finds runtime vulns SAST can't detect (ZAP, Burp Suite)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the principle of least privilege?
opts: Using the minimum number of servers
Granting users/services only the minimum permissions required to perform their function — nothing more
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Least privilege limits access rights to only what is necessary. A database service doesn't need network admin rights. A read-only app doesn't need write permissions. Reduces blast radius if credentials are compromised. Core security principle.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Least privilege = Grant minimum required permissions only — limits damage from compromised accounts

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures)?
opts: A publicly disclosed cybersecurity vulnerability with a standardized identifier (CVE-YEAR-NUMBER) from the MITRE CVE database
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: CVE is a catalog of publicly disclosed security vulnerabilities. Each entry gets a unique ID (e.g., CVE-2021-44228 = Log4Shell). CVEs have CVSS scores (0-10) indicating severity. Used by vulnerability scanners to identify known vulnerabilities in software.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: CVE = Public security vulnerability ID — CVE-YEAR-NUMBER; CVSS score = severity (0-10)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is image scanning in Docker security?
opts: Analyzing container images for known CVEs, malicious content, exposed secrets, and misconfigured packages before deployment
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Container image scanning checks images against CVE databases and security policies before deployment. Tools: Trivy, Clair, Snyk, AWS ECR scanning. Should be integrated into CI/CD to prevent deploying vulnerable images to production.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Image scanning = Check container image for CVEs + secrets before deployment — Trivy, Snyk, Clair

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What are Kubernetes RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) roles?
opts: Kubernetes authorization mechanism using roles (permissions) and role bindings to control which users/service accounts can perform which actions on which resources
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: RBAC controls access to Kubernetes API. Role/ClusterRole defines permissions (verbs: get, list, create, delete on resources). RoleBinding/ClusterRoleBinding assigns roles to subjects (users, groups, service accounts). Always use least privilege.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: K8s RBAC = Role (permissions) + RoleBinding (assign to user/SA) — control API access

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is secret management in DevOps and why is it critical?
opts: Securely storing, accessing, and rotating sensitive credentials (API keys, passwords, certificates) outside of application code and version control
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Hardcoded secrets in code/repos are a top security risk. Secret management tools (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault) store secrets encrypted, control access with policies, audit access, and support automatic rotation.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Secret management = Store credentials outside code — Vault/AWS Secrets Manager/Azure Key Vault

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a WAF (Web Application Firewall)?
opts: A security tool that monitors and filters HTTP traffic between a web application and the internet, blocking attacks like SQL injection, XSS, and CSRF
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: WAF inspects HTTP/HTTPS traffic and blocks application-layer attacks (OWASP Top 10: SQL injection, XSS, CSRF, RCE). Operates at Layer 7 (Application). Examples: AWS WAF, Cloudflare WAF, ModSecurity. Complements but doesn't replace code-level security.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: WAF = Layer 7 firewall — blocks SQL injection, XSS, CSRF in HTTP traffic (AWS WAF, Cloudflare)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a security token/JWT (JSON Web Token)?
opts: A compact, URL-safe token format for securely transmitting claims (user identity, permissions) between parties as a digitally signed JSON object
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: JWT has three parts (Header.Payload.Signature) base64-encoded. Header = algorithm type, Payload = claims (user ID, roles, expiry), Signature = HMAC or RSA signature for verification. Used for stateless authentication in APIs and microservices.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: JWT = Header.Payload.Signature — stateless auth token; signature verifies tampering

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the OWASP Top 10?
opts: A list of top 10 security tools
A regularly updated list of the 10 most critical web application security risks, including SQL injection, broken authentication, and XSS
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: OWASP Top 10 is the standard awareness document for web app security. Current top risks include: Broken Access Control, Cryptographic Failures, Injection (SQL/NoSQL), Insecure Design, Security Misconfiguration, Vulnerable Components, and more.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: OWASP Top 10 = Top web app security risks — Injection, Broken Auth, IDOR, Misconfig (must-know list)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is serverless computing?
opts: A cloud execution model where the cloud provider manages infrastructure — developers only write and deploy code (functions) and pay only per execution
A model where servers are never rebooted
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Serverless (FaaS) means you write functions, upload code, and the cloud runs it on demand. No server provisioning, patching, or capacity planning. Auto-scales to zero. Pay per invocation. Examples: AWS Lambda, Azure Functions, Google Cloud Functions.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Serverless = No server management — write code, cloud runs it; pay per execution, auto-scales to 0

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Kubernetes Operator?
opts: A method of packaging, deploying, and managing Kubernetes applications using custom controllers that extend Kubernetes API for domain-specific automation
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Operators extend Kubernetes to automate complex stateful application management (like deploying and operating databases, Kafka, Prometheus). They use Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and custom controllers to encode operational knowledge as code.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: K8s Operator = Custom controller + CRD — encodes app operational knowledge (day-2 ops)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Service Discovery in microservices?
opts: The process by which microservices automatically detect and connect to each other's network locations without hardcoded IP addresses
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: In dynamic environments, service IPs change constantly. Service discovery allows services to find each other automatically. Client-side discovery (Eureka) or server-side (Kubernetes DNS/Service). K8s provides built-in service discovery via DNS (service-name.namespace.svc.cluster.local).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Service Discovery = Auto-find service endpoints — K8s DNS resolves service-name.namespace.svc.cluster.local

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a circuit breaker pattern in microservices?
opts: A resilience pattern that stops calling a failing service after a threshold of failures, allowing it to recover, preventing cascading failures
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Circuit breaker (like Hystrix, Resilience4j) monitors calls to external services. After N failures, it 'opens' and returns fast error responses without actually calling the failing service. After a timeout, it tests if service recovered. Prevents cascade failures.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Circuit Breaker = Stop calling failing service after N failures — prevent cascade failures (Hystrix/Resilience4j)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Kubernetes Liveness Probe vs Readiness Probe?
opts: Liveness probe detects if a container is alive (restart if fails); Readiness probe detects if a container is ready to serve traffic (remove from service if fails)
Liveness is for startup; Readiness is for shutdown
Liveness checks network; Readiness checks storage
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Liveness: if fails, kubelet restarts the container — for detecting deadlocks or crashed apps. Readiness: if fails, pod removed from Service endpoints (no traffic routed) — for apps still loading config, warming cache, or temporarily overloaded.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Liveness = restart dead container; Readiness = remove from traffic if not ready (warm-up/overload)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Kubernetes resource requests vs limits?
opts: Request is minimum memory; limit is maximum network bandwidth
Requests are the minimum CPU/memory guaranteed for scheduling; limits are the maximum CPU/memory a container can use
Requests are for storage; limits are for compute
They are both the same — just different names
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Requests: scheduler uses this to find a node with sufficient capacity. Guaranteed allocation for the container. Limits: maximum resource the container can use. If CPU exceeds limit, it is throttled. If memory exceeds limit, the container is OOM killed.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Request = guaranteed minimum (for scheduling); Limit = max allowed (exceed → throttle/OOM kill)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Helm Chart?
opts: A collection of files defining a Kubernetes application deployment — parameterized YAML templates for consistent, repeatable deployments
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: A Helm Chart is a package containing all Kubernetes manifest templates + default values (values.yaml). Users can override values at install time. Charts can be versioned, shared via chart repositories, and managed like software packages.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Helm Chart = K8s app package — YAML templates + values.yaml; deploy with 'helm install'

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is artifact management in CI/CD?
opts: Storing and versioning build outputs (JARs, Docker images, npm packages) in a repository for consistent deployment across environments
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Artifact repositories (Nexus, JFrog Artifactory, AWS ECR, GitHub Packages) store build outputs with versions. CI pushes artifacts; CD pulls them for deployment. Ensures the exact same build artifact deployed to dev is deployed to production.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Artifact management = Store versioned build outputs — same artifact from dev to prod (Nexus/Artifactory)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a pipeline as code?
opts: Writing application code in a CI server
Defining the CI/CD pipeline configuration in a file committed to the source repository (Jenkinsfile, .gitlab-ci.yml, .github/workflows/) enabling version control of the pipeline
Using code to generate pipeline diagrams
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Pipeline as Code (PaC) stores pipeline definitions in version control alongside application code. Benefits: version history, code review of pipeline changes, reproducibility across branches. Files: Jenkinsfile, .gitlab-ci.yml, GitHub Actions YAML.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Pipeline as Code = Pipeline config in SCM — Jenkinsfile/.gitlab-ci.yml/.github/workflows/

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a deployment gate in CI/CD?
opts: A checkpoint in a deployment pipeline requiring automated or manual approval before the pipeline proceeds to the next environment
A firewall rule in deployment network
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Deployment gates (also called approval gates) are checkpoints in CD pipelines. They can require: manual approval from a team member, automated quality checks (test pass rate, code coverage, security scan results), or time-based windows before promoting to the next environment.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Deployment Gate = Pipeline checkpoint — manual/automated approval before promoting to next env

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Test-Driven Development (TDD)?
opts: A development practice where tests are written before the code — code is written only to make failing tests pass (Red-Green-Refactor cycle)
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: TDD cycle: Red (write a failing test), Green (write minimal code to pass), Refactor (improve code while keeping tests green). Benefits: better design, built-in test coverage, faster debugging. Common in unit testing with JUnit, pytest, Jest.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: TDD = Write test first → code to pass → refactor (Red-Green-Refactor cycle)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is code coverage and what is a good target?
opts: The total lines of code in a project
The percentage of code executed by automated tests — commonly targeted at 70-80% for most projects
The number of developers who reviewed the code
The percentage of code deployed to production
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Code coverage measures how much of your code is executed during tests. Tools: JaCoCo (Java), coverage.py (Python), Istanbul (JS). 100% coverage is not always the goal — focus on critical paths. 70-80% is commonly recommended; 80%+ for critical systems.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Code Coverage = % of code run by tests — 70-80% target; use JaCoCo/coverage.py/Istanbul

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Docker Swarm?
opts: Docker's native container orchestration tool for managing a cluster of Docker hosts
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Docker Swarm is Docker's built-in clustering and orchestration solution. It turns a pool of Docker hosts into a single virtual host. Less complex than Kubernetes but fewer features. Uses Swarm services, stacks, and overlay networks.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Docker Swarm = Docker's native clustering — simpler than K8s but fewer features

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the purpose of 'docker exec -it <container> /bin/bash'?
opts: Builds a container from a Dockerfile
Opens an interactive terminal session inside a running container
Exports a container as an image
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: 'docker exec' runs a command in a running container. -i (interactive) keeps stdin open, -t allocates a TTY. Combined, -it enables an interactive shell session. /bin/bash opens the bash shell. Useful for debugging inside containers.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: docker exec -it <container> /bin/bash = Interactive shell inside running container — for debugging

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Docker overlay network?
opts: A multi-host network that enables containers on different Docker hosts to communicate securely
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Overlay networks enable container communication across multiple Docker hosts (in Swarm or Kubernetes). Traffic is encapsulated using VXLAN. Required for multi-host container deployments. Docker Swarm creates overlay networks automatically for services.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Overlay network = Multi-host container networking using VXLAN encapsulation

it_infrastructure_devops.q: How do you reduce Docker image size?
opts: Use multi-stage builds, minimal base images (Alpine), combine RUN commands, and remove package cache in same RUN layer
Add more layers to the Dockerfile
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Key techniques: 1) Use alpine or distroless base images 2) Multi-stage builds (copy only artifacts) 3) Chain RUN commands with && to reduce layers 4) Clean up apt/yum cache in the same RUN layer (apt-get clean) 5) Use .dockerignore to exclude build artifacts.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Reduce image size: Alpine base + multi-stage + chain RUN + clean cache + .dockerignore

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the difference between 'docker stop' and 'docker kill'?
opts: docker stop sends SIGTERM (graceful shutdown, waits 10s); docker kill sends SIGKILL (immediate forced termination)
docker stop is for images; docker kill is for containers
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: 'docker stop' sends SIGTERM to the container's main process, allowing it to handle the signal and shut down gracefully. After 10 seconds, it sends SIGKILL. 'docker kill' sends SIGKILL immediately, bypassing graceful shutdown.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: docker stop = SIGTERM (graceful, 10s timeout); docker kill = SIGKILL (immediate force kill)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the Kubernetes control plane?
opts: The worker nodes in a cluster
The set of components that manage the cluster: API server, etcd, controller manager, and scheduler
The container runtime on each node
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: The control plane manages the cluster state: API Server (entry point), etcd (state store), Controller Manager (reconciliation loops for deployments, nodes, etc.), and Scheduler (pod-to-node assignment). In managed K8s (EKS/GKE/AKS), cloud manages the control plane.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: K8s Control Plane = API Server + etcd + Controller Manager + Scheduler — brain of the cluster

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is kube-proxy in Kubernetes?
opts: A proxy for the Kubernetes API server
A network component on each node that maintains network rules for pod communication and implements Kubernetes Service routing
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: kube-proxy runs on each node and maintains iptables/ipvs rules for routing traffic to pods. It implements the Kubernetes Service concept — when a Service IP receives traffic, kube-proxy routes it to the correct pod endpoints.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: kube-proxy = Node network agent — implements Service routing via iptables/ipvs rules

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Kubernetes Job vs CronJob?
opts: A Job runs a task to completion once; a CronJob runs a Job on a scheduled interval (like Unix cron)
A Job is for services; a CronJob is for databases
A Job is for stateful apps; a CronJob is for stateless apps
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Job: creates one or more pods, runs them to completion, and tracks successful completions. Used for batch processing, data migrations. CronJob: runs Jobs on a schedule using cron syntax (e.g., '0 3 * * *' = 3 AM daily). Used for scheduled backups, reports.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Job = run to completion once; CronJob = scheduled Job using cron syntax

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What does 'kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml' do vs 'kubectl create -f'?
opts: apply creates or updates existing resources (declarative, idempotent); create only creates new resources and fails if they already exist
apply is for deployments; create is for services
apply is for production; create is for development
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: 'kubectl apply' is idempotent — creates resources if they don't exist, updates them if they do. Tracks changes. Preferred for declarative, GitOps workflows. 'kubectl create' only works for new resources — errors if resource already exists.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: kubectl apply = idempotent create+update (declarative preferred); kubectl create = create only (errors if exists)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Kubernetes LimitRange?
opts: A limit on cluster node count
A policy that sets default and maximum CPU/memory requests and limits for containers in a namespace
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: LimitRange defines constraints on resource allocation per pod/container in a namespace. It sets default requests/limits for containers that don't specify them, and maximum/minimum bounds. Prevents pods from consuming excessive resources in a namespace.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: LimitRange = Namespace-level default and max CPU/memory constraints for pods/containers

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Terraform Cloud?
opts: HashiCorp's managed service for running Terraform — provides remote state, team collaboration, run history, and Sentinel policies
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Terraform Cloud is HashiCorp's SaaS platform for teams using Terraform. Features: remote state management, collaborative runs, version history, cost estimation, Sentinel policy enforcement, and VCS integration for automated runs on PR.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Terraform Cloud = HashiCorp SaaS for Terraform teams — remote state, collaboration, policy enforcement

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Terraform output?
opts: A value exported from a Terraform module or configuration that can be queried or used by other configurations
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Output values expose information from your Terraform configuration — like an EC2 instance's public IP or a VPC ID. Use 'terraform output <name>' to query them. Child modules can return outputs to parent modules for use in resource configurations.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Terraform output = Expose values from configuration — IPs, IDs — queryable by 'terraform output'

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Terraform's 'depends_on' meta-argument?
opts: Specifies which provider version to use
Explicitly defines resource dependencies when Terraform cannot automatically detect them from resource references
Sets the order of variable processing
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Terraform usually infers dependencies from resource references automatically. 'depends_on' explicitly declares that one resource must be created before another when there's no direct reference relationship. Use sparingly as it can slow plans.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: depends_on = Explicit resource ordering — use when Terraform can't auto-detect dependency

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the difference between 'terraform.tfvars' and variables.tf?
opts: variables.tf declares input variables (name, type, description, default); terraform.tfvars provides actual values for those variables
terraform.tfvars is for secrets; variables.tf is for non-sensitive values
variables.tf is for outputs; terraform.tfvars is for inputs
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: variables.tf defines the structure: variable 'region' { type = string, description = '...' }. terraform.tfvars (or *.auto.tfvars) provides the actual values: region = 'us-east-1'. Separation allows reusing variable declarations with different values per environment.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: variables.tf = declare variable schema; terraform.tfvars = provide variable values per environment

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the purpose of Jenkins Shared Libraries?
opts: Reusable Groovy code stored in a Git repo that can be imported by multiple Jenkins pipelines to eliminate code duplication
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Shared Libraries allow teams to define common pipeline functions, steps, and utilities in a central Git repository. Pipelines import them with '@Library'. Reduces duplication, enforces standards, and allows pipeline code to be updated centrally.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Jenkins Shared Library = Central Groovy code for reuse across pipelines — eliminate duplication

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Jenkins Declarative Pipeline's 'environment' directive?
opts: Defines environment variables available to all steps in the pipeline or specific stage
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: The 'environment' directive in Declarative Pipeline defines key-value pairs as environment variables. Variables defined at the top level are available to all stages; defined inside a stage, they're stage-scoped. Supports credentials() helper for secret injection.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Jenkins environment directive = Define pipeline env vars; use credentials() for secret injection

it_infrastructure_devops.q: How does Jenkins handle parallel stages?
opts: Jenkins cannot run stages in parallel
Using the 'parallel' directive inside a stage, Jenkins executes multiple branches simultaneously on available agents, reducing total pipeline time
Jenkins queues stages for sequential execution only
Parallel is only available in Scripted Pipeline
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: The 'parallel' directive in Declarative Pipeline allows running multiple stage branches simultaneously. Each branch can run on the same or different agents. Dramatically reduces pipeline time for independent tasks (e.g., running tests on multiple OS platforms simultaneously).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Jenkins parallel = Run stage branches simultaneously — reduces pipeline time for independent tasks

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is an Ansible ad-hoc command?
opts: A one-liner command using ansible to perform a task on managed hosts without writing a playbook — for quick, one-time tasks
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Ad-hoc commands run a single Ansible module directly from the command line without a playbook. Example: 'ansible all -m ping' checks connectivity; 'ansible webservers -m apt -a "name=nginx state=present" -b' installs nginx on webservers.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Ad-hoc command = One-liner Ansible module execution — 'ansible <hosts> -m <module> -a <args>'

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the Ansible 'when' conditional?
opts: A conditional statement that controls whether a task executes based on a variable value or registered result
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: The 'when' clause conditionally runs a task based on variable values, facts, or registered results. Example: 'when: ansible_os_family == "Debian"' runs only on Debian-family hosts. Supports Jinja2 expressions and boolean logic.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: when: = Ansible task condition — run task only when expression is true (Jinja2 expressions)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is 'ansible_facts' in Ansible?
opts: A list of Ansible playbook failures
System information automatically gathered by Ansible at the start of a play — OS, IP addresses, CPU, memory, and more
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Facts are variables automatically collected by Ansible's 'setup' module at play start. They include: OS type (ansible_os_family), IP addresses (ansible_default_ipv4.address), memory (ansible_memtotal_mb), CPU count, and more. Disable with 'gather_facts: false' for speed.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: ansible_facts = Auto-gathered system info — OS, IPs, memory, CPU; disable with gather_facts: false

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Ansible Tower (AWX)?
opts: An Ansible plugin for cloud management
A web-based enterprise UI for Ansible providing role-based access control, job scheduling, real-time output, and centralized inventory management
An Ansible module for tower servers
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Ansible Tower (commercial) / AWX (open source) provides a web UI and REST API for Ansible. Key features: visual dashboards, RBAC for playbook execution, job scheduling, credential storage, real-time job output, and integration with LDAP/AD.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Ansible Tower/AWX = Web UI for Ansible — RBAC, scheduling, credential mgmt, REST API

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is AWS ECS?
opts: Amazon Elastic Container Service — a fully managed container orchestration service for running Docker containers on AWS
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: ECS is AWS's native container orchestration. Runs containers on EC2 (self-managed nodes) or Fargate (serverless). Integrates natively with ALB, IAM, ECR, CloudWatch. Simpler than EKS (Kubernetes) but less portable. Fargate removes node management.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: ECS = AWS native container orchestration — EC2 launch type or Fargate (serverless nodes)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is AWS Fargate?
opts: A serverless compute engine for containers that removes the need to provision and manage EC2 instances — works with ECS and EKS
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Fargate is a serverless compute engine for ECS and EKS. You specify container CPU/memory requirements; Fargate provisions the underlying compute. No EC2 instances to manage, patch, or scale. Pay per vCPU and memory used per second.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Fargate = Serverless container compute — no EC2 management; pay per vCPU+memory per second

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is an AWS ALB (Application Load Balancer) vs NLB (Network Load Balancer)?
opts: ALB is older; NLB is newer
ALB operates at Layer 7 (HTTP/HTTPS) with content-based routing; NLB operates at Layer 4 (TCP/UDP) with ultra-low latency and static IP support
ALB is for internal traffic; NLB is for external traffic
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: ALB (Layer 7): routes based on URL path, hostname, headers, query string. Used for HTTP microservices. NLB (Layer 4): handles millions of requests per second with ultra-low latency. Used for TCP/UDP apps, gaming, IoT. NLB provides static IP; ALB does not.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: ALB = L7 HTTP routing (path/host-based); NLB = L4 TCP/UDP ultra-low latency with static IP

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is AWS CloudTrail?
opts: A service that records all API calls and account activity across AWS services for security analysis, compliance auditing, and troubleshooting
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: CloudTrail logs every API call made in your AWS account (who, what, when, where). Stored in S3. Used for security investigations (who deleted that resource?), compliance auditing, and change tracking. Enable CloudTrail in all regions and log to a centralized account.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: CloudTrail = AWS API audit log — records every API call; who/what/when/where for compliance

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is an AWS Route 53 Alias record?
opts: A CNAME record for AWS services
A Route 53-specific record type that maps a domain to an AWS resource (ELB, CloudFront, S3) without the CNAME restriction on zone apex records
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Alias records are Route 53-specific and can point to AWS resources (ELBs, CloudFront, S3, API Gateway). Unlike CNAME, Alias records work at zone apex (example.com, not subdomain). They're free (no per-query charge) and automatically update when resource IP changes.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Route 53 Alias = Like CNAME but works at zone apex, free, auto-updates for AWS resources

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is AWS S3 versioning?
opts: A feature that keeps multiple versions of an object in the same bucket — enables recovery from accidental deletion or overwrites
A feature for S3 lifecycle policies
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: S3 versioning preserves every version of every object. Accidental deletions just add a 'delete marker' — previous versions remain recoverable. Required for S3 Object Lock (WORM compliance). Increases storage cost — use lifecycle policies to manage old versions.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: S3 Versioning = Keep all object versions — recover from accidental delete/overwrite (adds delete markers)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Azure Service Bus?
opts: A fully managed enterprise message broker supporting queues and topics/subscriptions for decoupling distributed applications
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Azure Service Bus is a managed message broker for reliable async communication between applications. Queues: point-to-point (one sender, one receiver). Topics/Subscriptions: pub/sub (one sender, multiple receivers). Supports AMQP, HTTPS, and sessions.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Azure Service Bus = Managed message broker — Queues (p2p) and Topics/Subscriptions (pub/sub)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Azure Container Registry (ACR)?
opts: A private Docker registry service in Azure for storing and managing container images and Helm charts
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: ACR is Azure's private container registry. Store, manage, and deploy Docker container images and Helm charts. Integrates natively with AKS, Azure Container Instances, and Azure DevOps pipelines. Supports geo-replication for global availability.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: ACR = Azure private container registry — store images/Helm charts; integrates with AKS/pipelines

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Azure Logic Apps?
opts: A workflow automation service for integrating apps, data, and services using a visual designer without writing code
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Logic Apps automate workflows and business processes by connecting apps, data, services, and systems using pre-built connectors (300+). Designed for non-developers using visual drag-and-drop. Use cases: data integration, notifications, approval workflows.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Azure Logic Apps = Visual workflow automation — 300+ connectors, no code required

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What are the three pillars of observability?
opts: Logs, metrics, and traces — together providing comprehensive visibility into system behavior
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: The three pillars: Logs (timestamped event records — what happened), Metrics (numerical measurements over time — how much/how many), Traces (request flow across services — distributed tracing). Together they enable finding, understanding, and resolving production issues.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Observability pillars = Logs (events) + Metrics (numbers) + Traces (request flow)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is distributed tracing?
opts: Tracking file changes across distributed systems
A technique that tracks a request as it flows through multiple microservices, identifying latency hotspots and errors across service boundaries
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Distributed tracing follows a request across multiple services using trace IDs and span IDs. Each service adds a span to the trace. Tools like Jaeger, Zipkin, and AWS X-Ray visualize the trace to identify which service is slow or failing.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Distributed tracing = Track requests across microservices — trace ID + spans; Jaeger/Zipkin/X-Ray

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the difference between monitoring and observability?
opts: Monitoring tells you when something is wrong (predefined metrics/alerts); observability lets you understand why it's wrong (exploration through logs/metrics/traces)
Monitoring is for development; observability is for production
Monitoring is manual; observability is automated
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Monitoring = watching known failure modes through predefined dashboards and alerts. Observability = ability to ask arbitrary questions about system state using logs, metrics, and traces. Observability is more powerful — you can debug unknown problems without predefined alerts.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Monitoring = detect known problems; Observability = understand any problem (explore unknowns)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Prometheus Histogram metric?
opts: A metric that only tracks maximum values
A metric that samples observations and counts them in configurable buckets — used for measuring request latency distributions and response size
A metric for tracking running totals
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Histogram samples observations (e.g., HTTP request duration) and counts them in pre-defined buckets (e.g., 0.1s, 0.5s, 1s, 5s). Also provides _count (total observations) and _sum (sum of all values). Enables percentile calculations with histogram_quantile().
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Histogram = Bucket-based distribution — use histogram_quantile() for p99 latency calculation

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a monolithic vs microservices architecture trade-off?
opts: Monolith is always worse; microservices are always better
Monolith: simpler to develop/deploy/debug; Microservices: independent scaling/deployment but complex distributed system management
Monolith uses more resources; microservices use less
Monolith is for large teams; microservices for small teams
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Monolith: all in one deployable unit — simple local development, easy testing, but scaling/deployment of entire app for small changes. Microservices: independent services — independent scaling and deployment, but network calls, distributed tracing, service discovery complexity.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Monolith = simple but scale-as-one; Microservices = independent scale/deploy but distributed complexity

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is chaos engineering?
opts: Intentionally injecting failures into a production system to proactively discover weaknesses before they cause real outages
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Chaos engineering (pioneered by Netflix with Chaos Monkey) deliberately introduces failures (terminate instances, inject latency, corrupt packets) in controlled experiments to verify that systems handle failure gracefully. Improves resilience and validates runbooks.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Chaos Engineering = Intentional failure injection — verify resilience before real outages (Chaos Monkey)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a feature flag (feature toggle)?
opts: A technique that enables/disables application features without deploying new code — allows dark launches, A/B testing, and gradual rollouts
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Feature flags wrap new functionality in conditional logic controlled by a configuration system (not code deployment). Benefits: deploy dark (off for all users), enable for specific users/cohorts for A/B testing, kill switch for problematic features. Tools: LaunchDarkly, Unleash, AWS AppConfig.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Feature Flag = Code is deployed but feature toggled on/off via config — dark launches, A/B, kill switch

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is zero-downtime deployment?
opts: A deployment strategy ensuring the application remains available throughout the update process with no service interruption
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Zero-downtime deployment keeps the app available during updates. Strategies: Rolling update (replace pods gradually), Blue-Green (instant traffic switch), Canary (gradual traffic shift). Requires health checks, graceful shutdown, and readiness probes.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Zero-downtime = App stays available during deploy — Rolling/Blue-Green/Canary + readiness probes

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is container resource isolation?
opts: The use of Linux kernel namespaces (isolate processes, network, filesystem) and cgroups (limit CPU/memory) to provide isolated environments for containers
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Containers use Linux namespaces (PID, network, mount, UTS, IPC, user) for isolation — each container sees only its own processes and network. cgroups (control groups) limit and account for CPU, memory, and I/O resources. This is lighter than VMs but less isolated.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Container isolation = namespaces (what you see) + cgroups (what you get) — lighter than VMs

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the difference between container and virtual machine?
opts: Containers are larger; VMs are faster
Containers share the host OS kernel (lightweight, fast start); VMs run a full OS on a hypervisor (stronger isolation, slower start, more resources)
Containers require a hypervisor; VMs do not
VMs use Docker; containers use VMware
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: VMs virtualize hardware via hypervisor — each has full OS, stronger isolation, but higher overhead (GBs, minutes to start). Containers share host OS kernel, only package app + dependencies — lightweight (MBs, seconds to start), less isolation (kernel shared).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Container = shared kernel, lightweight (MB, seconds); VM = full OS, isolated (GB, minutes)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is mTLS (Mutual TLS)?
opts: Authentication where both client and server verify each other's certificates — used in service mesh and zero-trust architectures
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: mTLS requires both parties to present and verify certificates. In standard TLS, only the server authenticates. In mTLS, both client and server present certificates — ensuring both are who they claim to be. Used in service mesh (Istio), zero-trust networks, and API security.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: mTLS = Mutual authentication — both client AND server verify certificates (Istio/zero-trust)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM)?
opts: A comprehensive inventory of all components, libraries, and dependencies in a software build — enabling vulnerability management and supply chain security
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: SBOM is a formal list of all components in a software product — direct and transitive dependencies with versions. After Log4Shell and SolarWinds attacks, SBOMs are critical for supply chain security. US executive order requires SBOMs for federal software. Tools: Syft, CycloneDX, SPDX.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: SBOM = Full dependency inventory — identifies vulnerable components; required for software supply chain security

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a zero-trust security model?
opts: Trusting all traffic on the internal network
A security model that assumes no trust by default — every access request must be authenticated, authorized, and continuously validated regardless of network location
A model with zero security controls
A model that trusts only internal users
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Zero-trust replaces 'trust but verify' with 'never trust, always verify.' Every request (even internal) is authenticated, authorized, and logged. Principles: verify identity (MFA), least privilege access, assume breach, encrypt all traffic (mTLS). Implemented via identity providers, service mesh, and micro-segmentation.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Zero-Trust = Never trust, always verify — authenticate every request, least privilege, assume breach

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the purpose of EXPOSE instruction in a Dockerfile?
opts: Actually opens a port on the host
Documents which port the container listens on at runtime — it does not publish the port
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: EXPOSE is documentation — it informs Docker and users which port the containerized application listens on. It does NOT publish the port to the host. Use 'docker run -p host_port:container_port' to publish. EXPOSE makes the intent clear for image consumers.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: EXPOSE = Documentation only — declares intended port; -p flag actually publishes it to host

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Docker named volume vs bind mount?
opts: Named volume is managed by Docker (stored in Docker's storage area); bind mount maps a specific host directory path directly into the container
Named volume is faster; bind mount is more secure
Bind mount is for databases; named volume is for apps
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Named volumes are managed by Docker — stored in /var/lib/docker/volumes/. Docker handles creation, backup, and migration. Bind mounts map a specific host path into the container — tightly coupled to host filesystem. Named volumes are preferred for production data.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Named volume = Docker managed storage; Bind mount = specific host path — named volumes preferred for prod

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What does 'docker image prune' do?
opts: Removes only dangling images (untagged images not referenced by any container)
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: 'docker image prune' removes dangling images — images with no tag and not referenced by any container. These are left over from builds when a newer image replaced the tag. Add -a to also remove all unused images (not just dangling ones).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: docker image prune = Remove dangling (untagged) images; -a flag removes all unused images

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is container orchestration?
opts: Automated management of containerized applications across a cluster — handling deployment, scaling, networking, load balancing, and self-healing
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Container orchestration automates: deploying containers to appropriate nodes, scaling based on load, restarting failed containers (self-healing), service discovery, load balancing, rolling updates, and resource management. Kubernetes is the dominant orchestration platform.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Container Orchestration = Automate deploy/scale/heal containers across cluster — Kubernetes dominates

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the Docker build context?
opts: The set of files and directories at the specified path sent to the Docker daemon when running 'docker build' — used to satisfy COPY and ADD instructions
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: The build context is the directory (usually '.') sent to the Docker daemon when running 'docker build'. All files in this directory are available for COPY/ADD instructions. Large build contexts slow down builds — use .dockerignore to exclude unnecessary files.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Build context = Files sent to Docker daemon for build — use .dockerignore to keep it small

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Kubernetes NetworkPolicy?
opts: A policy for cluster node network configuration
A specification defining rules for how pods are allowed to communicate with each other and with external endpoints
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: NetworkPolicy defines ingress and egress rules controlling which pods can communicate with which other pods or external endpoints. By default, all pod-to-pod communication is allowed. NetworkPolicy requires a CNI plugin that supports it (Calico, Cilium, Weave).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: NetworkPolicy = Pod firewall rules — control pod-to-pod and pod-to-external traffic (needs CNI support)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the difference between a Kubernetes Deployment and ReplicaSet?
opts: Deployment manages ReplicaSets — it adds rolling update and rollback capabilities on top of the basic pod replication that ReplicaSet provides
Deployment is for stateful apps; ReplicaSet is for stateless
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: ReplicaSet ensures N replicas of a pod are running but has no update strategy. Deployment wraps ReplicaSet, adding rolling update orchestration (manages old and new ReplicaSets during updates) and rollback capability. Always use Deployment, not ReplicaSet directly.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Deployment = ReplicaSet + rolling updates + rollback — always use Deployment, not raw ReplicaSet

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is 'kubectl rollout undo deployment/<name>' used for?
opts: Rolls back the deployment to the previous revision — useful when a new deployment causes issues
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: 'kubectl rollout undo' reverts a Deployment to its previous revision. Kubernetes keeps a rollout history (configurable via revisionHistoryLimit). You can also roll back to a specific revision with '--to-revision=N'. Essential for quick recovery from bad deploys.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: kubectl rollout undo = Rollback deployment to previous revision — quick recovery from bad deploy

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Kubernetes PodDisruptionBudget (PDB)?
opts: A policy that limits the number of pods of a replicated application that can be down simultaneously during voluntary disruptions like node drains or upgrades
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: PDB ensures high availability during voluntary disruptions (node maintenance, cluster upgrades). It specifies minAvailable (minimum pods that must be running) or maxUnavailable (maximum pods that can be down). Prevents node drain from taking down too many pods at once.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: PDB = Availability guarantee during disruptions — minAvailable or maxUnavailable during node drain/upgrades

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the Kubernetes Admission Controller?
opts: A plugin that intercepts requests to the Kubernetes API server before object persistence to validate, mutate, or reject them based on custom policies
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Admission controllers intercept API requests after authentication/authorization but before object storage. Validating: approve/reject (e.g., prevent privileged containers). Mutating: modify requests (e.g., inject sidecars, set defaults). Examples: PodSecurityAdmission, LimitRanger, OPA Gatekeeper.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Admission Controller = Intercept K8s API requests — validate/mutate before saving (OPA Gatekeeper, PSA)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Terraform's 'for_each' meta-argument?
opts: Creates multiple instances of a resource from a map or set, giving each instance a unique key — more flexible than count
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: 'for_each' creates a resource instance for each element in a map or set. Each instance is indexed by its key (not a number like 'count'). Deleting one item only destroys that specific instance — unlike count where removing middle element causes cascading rebuilds.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: for_each = Create resources from map/set — delete one item = only that instance destroyed (vs count)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the Terraform Registry?
opts: A public repository of providers and modules that Terraform can download and use
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: The Terraform Registry (registry.terraform.io) hosts public providers (AWS, Azure, GCP, Kubernetes, 3000+ providers) and modules (reusable infrastructure patterns). Modules are versioned and can be pinned. Private registries are available via Terraform Cloud.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Terraform Registry = Public hub for providers and modules — registry.terraform.io

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Terraform state locking?
opts: Encrypting the state file at rest
Preventing concurrent Terraform operations by locking the state file — prevents two engineers from running 'terraform apply' simultaneously and corrupting state
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: State locking prevents simultaneous state modifications. When using remote backends (S3+DynamoDB, Terraform Cloud, Azure Storage), Terraform locks state before operations and unlocks after. Prevents race conditions that could corrupt infrastructure state.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: State locking = Prevent concurrent applies — S3 uses DynamoDB for locks; Terraform Cloud handles it automatically

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is 'terraform fmt' used for?
opts: Automatically formats Terraform configuration files to the canonical style — consistent indentation and spacing
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: 'terraform fmt' rewrites Terraform config files to the canonical format defined by HashiCorp — consistent indentation (2 spaces), alignment of = signs, and proper spacing. Run it before committing to maintain consistent code style. CI pipelines often check 'terraform fmt -check'.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: terraform fmt = Auto-format .tf files to canonical style — run before commit; check in CI

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Jenkins Multibranch Pipeline?
opts: A pipeline that runs on multiple Jenkins servers
A pipeline project that automatically creates pipeline jobs for each branch in a source repository — uses Jenkinsfile from each branch
A pipeline for managing multiple applications
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Multibranch Pipeline automatically discovers branches (and PRs) in a Git repo and creates pipeline jobs for each using the branch's Jenkinsfile. New branches get pipelines automatically. Deleted branches get their jobs removed. Ideal for trunk-based development and PR validation.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Multibranch Pipeline = Auto-create pipeline per branch using that branch's Jenkinsfile

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the Jenkins workspace?
opts: A directory on the Jenkins agent where source code is checked out and build commands are executed for a specific job
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Each Jenkins job has a workspace directory on the agent where source code is checked out and build commands run. By default: $JENKINS_HOME/workspace/<job-name>. Multiple executors on the same agent get separate workspace directories. Clean with 'deleteDir()' or 'cleanWs()'.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Jenkins workspace = Agent directory where code is checked out and built — clean with cleanWs()

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Ansible's 'block' directive used for?
opts: A way to comment out tasks
Groups tasks together to apply shared attributes (when, become, tags) and enables error handling with rescue and always sections
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: 'block' groups related tasks together. Benefits: apply a single 'when' condition to multiple tasks, add 'rescue' for error handling (like try/catch), and 'always' for cleanup regardless of result. Makes playbooks cleaner and adds structured error recovery.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: block = Group tasks + error handling — block/rescue/always like try/catch/finally in Ansible

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Ansible's 'register' keyword?
opts: Captures the output (return value) of a task into a variable for use in subsequent tasks
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: 'register' saves a task's return value to a variable. The registered variable contains stdout, stderr, return code, and other metadata. Commonly used to check if a service is running, capture command output, or conditionally run subsequent tasks based on the result.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: register: var_name = Capture task output — use in 'when' conditions or debug output

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Ansible's 'delegate_to' used for?
opts: Runs a specific task on a different host than the one being targeted — useful for updating load balancers or triggering remote actions
Delegates task execution to a sub-process
Runs tasks in parallel on all hosts
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: 'delegate_to' redirects a task to run on a specific host instead of the current inventory host. Common uses: register a server with a load balancer (run against LB, not web server), update DNS, or trigger monitoring alerts from a management server.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: delegate_to: host = Run this task on a different host — e.g., update LB while configuring web servers

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is AWS SQS (Simple Queue Service)?
opts: A fully managed message queue service for decoupling and scaling distributed systems — supports Standard (at-least-once) and FIFO (exactly-once, ordered) queues
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: SQS is a managed message queue for async communication between services. Standard queues: maximum throughput, at-least-once delivery, best-effort ordering. FIFO queues: exactly-once processing, strict ordering, lower throughput (3000 msg/s). Common for decoupling microservices.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: SQS = Managed message queue — Standard (at-least-once) vs FIFO (exactly-once, ordered)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is AWS SNS (Simple Notification Service)?
opts: A managed pub/sub messaging service that fans out messages to multiple subscribers (SQS, Lambda, HTTP, email, SMS) simultaneously
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: SNS is a pub/sub service. Publishers send messages to topics; SNS fans out to all subscribers simultaneously. Subscribers can be SQS queues, Lambda functions, HTTP endpoints, email addresses, or SMS. Fan-out pattern: one SNS message → multiple SQS queues.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: SNS = Pub/Sub fan-out — one topic message → multiple subscribers (SQS, Lambda, HTTP, email, SMS)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is AWS Elastic Beanstalk?
opts: A PaaS service that automatically handles deployment, load balancing, auto-scaling, and monitoring — developers just upload application code
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Elastic Beanstalk is AWS's PaaS. Upload your application code (ZIP, WAR, Docker image), choose platform (Java, Python, Node.js, PHP, Docker), and Beanstalk handles EC2, load balancing, auto-scaling, and health monitoring. Full control over underlying resources if needed.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Elastic Beanstalk = AWS PaaS — upload code, AWS manages infra (EC2/ELB/ASG); full underlying access

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is AWS CodePipeline?
opts: A fully managed CI/CD service that automates the build, test, and deploy phases of the release process
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: CodePipeline orchestrates CI/CD workflows in AWS. Stages: Source (CodeCommit/GitHub/S3), Build (CodeBuild), Test, Deploy (CodeDeploy/ECS/Beanstalk). Integrates natively with AWS services. Competes with Jenkins for AWS-native deployments.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: CodePipeline = AWS-native CI/CD orchestration — Source → Build → Test → Deploy stages

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is AWS Systems Manager (SSM)?
opts: An AWS service for managing EC2 instances and on-premises servers — provides Session Manager (SSH without bastion), Patch Manager, Parameter Store, and automation
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: SSM provides operational management at scale. Key features: Session Manager (browser-based SSH, no port 22 needed), Patch Manager (automated OS patching), Parameter Store (centralized config/secrets), Run Command (execute scripts across fleets), and Automation (runbooks).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: SSM = AWS fleet management — Session Manager (no SSH key/bastion), Patch Manager, Parameter Store

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Azure Policy?
opts: A service for creating, assigning, and managing policies that enforce rules and effects on Azure resources to ensure compliance
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Azure Policy defines and enforces organizational standards. Policies evaluate resources for non-compliance. Effects: Deny (block non-compliant resource creation), Audit (log but allow), Append (add fields), DeployIfNotExists (auto-deploy compliant config). Used for governance.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Azure Policy = Governance guardrails — enforce standards; Deny/Audit/DeployIfNotExists effects

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Azure Managed Identity?
opts: An Azure AD identity automatically managed by Azure for services to authenticate to other Azure resources without storing credentials in code
A managed service account for on-premises
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Managed Identity eliminates the need to store credentials in code or config. Azure manages the identity lifecycle and token refresh. System-assigned: tied to a specific resource lifecycle. User-assigned: independent identity reusable across multiple resources.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Managed Identity = Azure service authenticates to other services without credentials in code

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the Azure Resource Group?
opts: A logical container for grouping related Azure resources that share the same lifecycle — deployed, managed, and deleted together
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Resource Groups are logical containers for organizing related Azure resources. Resources in a group typically share the same lifecycle. You can apply RBAC, tags, and policies at the resource group level. Deleting a resource group deletes all resources within it.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Resource Group = Logical container for related Azure resources — share lifecycle, RBAC, and tags

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Prometheus recording rule?
opts: A rule for recording alert history
A pre-computed expression result saved as a new time series — speeds up dashboards and complex queries by pre-aggregating expensive calculations
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Recording rules pre-compute frequently needed or computationally expensive PromQL expressions and save results as new time series. This speeds up dashboards that run complex aggregations on large datasets. Defined in rules files with the 'record' key.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Recording rule = Pre-compute expensive PromQL → save as new metric — faster dashboards

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Prometheus service discovery?
opts: A way for Prometheus to find its own configuration
A mechanism for Prometheus to automatically discover and scrape new targets as they appear in dynamic environments (Kubernetes, EC2, Consul) without manual config updates
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Prometheus supports dynamic service discovery for cloud/container environments. In Kubernetes, it uses the K8s API to discover pods and services with prometheus.io/scrape annotations. Also supports EC2, Consul, Azure, GCP, and others — no manual target config needed.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Service Discovery = Prometheus auto-discovers targets from K8s/EC2/Consul — no manual config

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is an SLI (Service Level Indicator)?
opts: A carefully defined quantitative measure of some aspect of service performance — the actual metric being measured (e.g., request latency, error rate, availability)
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: SLIs are the actual metrics you measure: availability (% of successful requests), latency (p99 < 200ms), error rate (errors / total requests), throughput (requests/second). SLOs are targets SET on top of SLIs. SLAs are contracts based on SLOs.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: SLI = The actual metric measured (latency/error rate/availability); SLO = target; SLA = contract

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is an error budget in SRE?
opts: A budget for fixing production bugs
The acceptable amount of downtime/errors remaining before violating an SLO — calculated as 1 - SLO target (e.g., 99.9% SLO = 0.1% error budget = 43.8 min/month)
A financial budget for error monitoring tools
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Error budget = 100% - SLO. If SLO is 99.9% uptime, you have 0.1% error budget = ~43.8 minutes/month of allowed downtime. If budget is exhausted, new feature work stops in favor of reliability. If budget remains, you can ship faster.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Error budget = 100% - SLO — exhausted budget stops feature work; remaining budget enables faster shipping

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is toil in SRE context?
opts: Hard, important work that improves systems
Manual, repetitive, automatable operational work that scales linearly with service growth — the opposite of engineering work that produces lasting value
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Toil (defined by Google SRE) is work that is: manual, repetitive, automatable, tactical, lacks enduring value, and scales linearly with service size. SRE teams aim to keep toil below 50% of work time. The rest is engineering work that reduces future toil.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Toil = Manual repetitive ops work that scales linearly — SRE targets <50% toil; rest = engineering

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a runbook in DevOps/SRE?
opts: A book of server hardware specifications
A documented set of step-by-step procedures for performing operational tasks or responding to incidents — ensures consistent and correct execution
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Runbooks document routine operational procedures (deployments, scaling, backups) and incident response steps. Good runbooks: clearly written, tested, versioned, and automated where possible. Modern runbooks are often Jupyter notebooks or automated scripts.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Runbook = Step-by-step operational procedure — routine tasks and incident response; automate where possible

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Mean Time To Recovery (MTTR)?
opts: The average time it takes to restore a service after a failure — a key reliability metric indicating incident response and recovery efficiency
Mean time to release new features
Mean time to resolve team conflicts
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: MTTR measures the average time from incident detection to service restoration. Lower MTTR = more resilient system. Improved by: better alerting (faster detection), runbooks (faster diagnosis), automated recovery (faster resolution), and post-mortems (prevent recurrence).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: MTTR = Avg time to restore service after failure — lower = more resilient; improve with automation/runbooks

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a post-mortem (incident retrospective) in SRE?
opts: A blameless analysis of an incident after resolution — identifying root cause, impact, contributing factors, and action items to prevent recurrence
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Post-mortems (blameless) analyze what happened, why, and how to prevent it. Key sections: timeline, root cause(s), contributing factors, impact, action items. Blameless culture focuses on fixing systems/processes, not blaming individuals. Shared widely to spread learning.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Post-mortem = Blameless incident analysis — timeline, root cause, action items, shared learning

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the difference between horizontal and vertical scaling?
opts: Horizontal uses more RAM; vertical adds more servers
Horizontal scaling adds more instances (scale out); vertical scaling adds more resources (CPU/RAM) to existing instances (scale up)
Horizontal is for databases; vertical is for web servers
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Horizontal scaling (scale out): add more servers/instances — better for stateless workloads, enables high availability, no single point of failure. Vertical scaling (scale up): add more CPU/RAM to existing server — simpler, but has hardware limits and causes downtime.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Horizontal = add more instances (scale out, no SPOF); Vertical = bigger instance (scale up, limited)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Infrastructure Immutability?
opts: A practice where servers are never modified after deployment — instead, new updated images are deployed and old ones are replaced
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Immutable infrastructure means servers are never patched or configured post-deployment. When changes are needed, a new image is built and new instances are deployed; old ones are terminated. Eliminates configuration drift, simplifies rollback, and improves consistency.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Immutable infra = Never modify servers — build new image, deploy new instances, terminate old

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a service registry?
opts: A DNS server for internal services
A database that stores network locations of service instances, enabling services to discover and connect to each other dynamically in microservice architectures
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Service registries (Consul, Eureka, etcd) maintain up-to-date lists of available service instances and their network locations. Services register on startup and deregister on shutdown. Clients query the registry to find available instances. Kubernetes has built-in service discovery via DNS.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Service Registry = Dynamic service location database — Consul/Eureka; K8s uses built-in DNS

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a reverse proxy vs a forward proxy?
opts: Forward proxy sits in front of clients (hides client identity from servers); reverse proxy sits in front of servers (hides server identity from clients, provides LB/SSL)
Forward proxy is for external traffic; reverse proxy is for internal
Forward proxy is faster; reverse proxy is more secure
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Forward proxy: clients use it to access the internet — hides client IP, used for web filtering/anonymity. Reverse proxy: sits in front of servers — clients see reverse proxy IP, not backend servers. Provides load balancing, SSL termination, caching. Examples: NGINX, HAProxy, Traefik.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Forward proxy = hides client (corporate web filter); Reverse proxy = hides servers (NGINX/HAProxy LB)
