it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the primary goal of ITIL Service Strategy?
opts: Define how IT services will be delivered
Align IT services with business objectives and define value creation
Plan the release of new services
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: ITIL Service Strategy aligns IT services with business objectives, defining perspective, position, plans, and patterns a provider needs to meet business outcomes.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Service Strategy = aligning IT with business to create value

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What are the three types of service providers in ITIL?
opts: Type I (Internal), Type II (Shared), Type III (External)
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: ITIL: Type I = Internal Service Provider (embedded in one business unit), Type II = Shared Services Unit (serves multiple units), Type III = External Service Provider (open market).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: ITIL service provider types: Type I=Internal, Type II=Shared, Type III=External

it_infrastructure_devops.q: Which ITIL process includes Budgeting, Accounting, and Charging?
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Financial Management involves Budgeting (plan spend), Accounting (track spend), and Charging (bill customers), enabling IT cost visibility and IT-as-a-business thinking.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Financial Management = Budgeting + Accounting + Charging

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Service Portfolio in ITIL?
opts: A list of all hardware assets
The complete set of services across all lifecycle stages: Pipeline, Catalog, and Retired
A catalog of services available to customers
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Service Portfolio = Pipeline (proposed/in development) + Service Catalog (live) + Retired Services. It represents the provider's commitments and investments across all lifecycle stages.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Service Portfolio = Pipeline + Catalog + Retired services

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What distinguishes the Service Catalog from the Service Portfolio?
opts: Catalog is internal; Portfolio is external
Service Catalog lists only active/live services available to customers; Portfolio covers all lifecycle stages
Portfolio is for customers; Catalog is for IT staff
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: The Service Catalog is a subset listing only services currently available to customers. The full Portfolio also includes pipeline (in development) and retired services.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Service Catalog = live services only; Service Portfolio = all lifecycle stages

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Demand Management in ITIL?
opts: Understanding and influencing customer demand for services and coordinating with Capacity Management to ensure sufficient capacity
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Demand Management understands patterns of business activity (PBAs) and user profiles to predict and influence demand, coordinating with Capacity Management for adequate supply.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Demand Management = understand + influence customer demand; coordinate with Capacity Management

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the Change Advisory Board (CAB)?
opts: A cross-functional group advising the Change Manager on assessing and authorising Normal Changes
A group that manages service catalog
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: The CAB advises the Change Manager by assessing, prioritising, and authorising Normal Changes. Membership includes IT managers, business reps, and technical experts.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: CAB = advisory group that assesses and authorises normal changes

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Known Error in ITIL?
opts: A problem with a documented root cause and a workaround or permanent fix, stored in the KEDB
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: A Known Error has a documented root cause and workaround. Stored in the Known Error Database (KEDB), they help resolve recurring incidents faster without re-investigating root cause.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Known Error = documented root cause + workaround; stored in KEDB

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What does DIKW stand for in ITIL Knowledge Management?
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: DIKW: Data (raw facts) > Information (processed data with context) > Knowledge (applied with experience) > Wisdom (correct decision-making). Foundation of ITIL Knowledge Management.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: DIKW = Data > Information > Knowledge > Wisdom

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the CSI 7-step improvement process first step?
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: CSI 7 steps: 1) Define what you SHOULD measure, 2) Define what you CAN measure, 3) Gather data, 4) Process data, 5) Analyse, 6) Present, 7) Implement improvement.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: CSI 7-step first step = Define what you SHOULD measure

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is IT Service Continuity Management (ITSCM)?
opts: Ensuring services are delivered within agreed cost
Managing risks and ensuring IT service recovery within agreed timescales after a disaster
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: ITSCM ensures IT services can be recovered within agreed timescales after a disaster. Includes risk assessment, Business Impact Analysis (BIA), recovery options, and continuity testing.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: ITSCM = recover IT services within agreed time after disruption

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What are CSFs and KPIs in ITIL?
opts: Critical Success Factors and Key Performance Indicators used to measure process effectiveness
Customer Satisfaction Forms and Key Process Indicators
Core System Functions and Key Process Information
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: CSFs (Critical Success Factors) = conditions that must exist for a process to succeed. KPIs (Key Performance Indicators) = specific measurable values tracking performance against targets.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: CSFs = what must go right; KPIs = how we measure if it did

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is an Operational Level Agreement (OLA)?
opts: Agreement between IT and external suppliers
Agreement between different IT teams within the same organisation to support SLA delivery
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: An OLA is an internal IT agreement (e.g., network team commits response times to service desk) to support delivery of SLAs to customers. OLAs underpin SLAs.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: OLA = internal IT team agreement that underpins SLAs

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the 4 Ps framework in ITIL Service Design?
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: 4 Ps of Service Design: People (skills/competencies), Products (technology), Processes (IT management processes), Partners/Suppliers (third-party relationships). All must be balanced.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Service Design 4 Ps = People, Products, Processes, Partners

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Release and Deployment Management responsible for?
opts: Planning, scheduling, controlling the build, test, and deployment of releases into production
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Release and Deployment Management plans and controls releases into live environments, ensuring releases are built, tested, and deployed to minimise impact on live services.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Release & Deployment = plan, build, test, deploy releases to production

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the Service Desk's role in ITIL?
opts: The single point of contact between IT and users for all service requests and incidents
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: The Service Desk is the SPOC (Single Point of Contact) for users. It logs, categorises, prioritises, and resolves or escalates incidents and service requests.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Service Desk = SPOC; logs and coordinates incidents and service requests

it_infrastructure_devops.q: Event Management in ITIL classifies events as:
opts: Informational, Warning, or Exception - triggering appropriate control actions
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Event Management monitors IT infrastructure events. Informational (no action needed), Warning (approaching threshold), Exception (requires action - may create incident).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Events: Informational, Warning, Exception; Exception triggers incident/alert

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What does ITIL Continual Service Improvement (CSI) use for iterative improvement?
opts: PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle for iterative improvement based on measurement and feedback
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: CSI uses the Deming PDCA cycle: Plan (design improvement), Do (implement), Check (measure results), Act (embed or correct). Enables evidence-based, iterative improvement.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: CSI uses PDCA (Deming Cycle) for iterative improvement

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the Service Design Package (SDP)?
opts: All aspects needed to take a service through remaining lifecycle stages: requirements, design, transition plan, acceptance criteria
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: SDP is produced for each new/changed IT service. Contains: business requirements, service design, organisational readiness assessment, service lifecycle plan, and acceptance criteria.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: SDP = comprehensive blueprint for a service through its entire lifecycle

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Service Level Management (SLM) responsible for?
opts: Negotiating, agreeing, documenting, and monitoring Service Level Agreements with customers
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: SLM negotiates and monitors SLAs with customers, OLAs with internal teams, and UCs (Underpinning Contracts) with suppliers. Ensures services meet agreed quality targets.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: SLM = negotiate + monitor SLAs; manages OLAs (internal) and UCs (suppliers) too

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What does HVAC stand for in data centers?
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: HVAC (Heating, Ventilation, Air Conditioning) systems maintain optimal temperature and humidity in data centers, preventing equipment overheating which is a leading cause of hardware failure.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: HVAC = Heating Ventilation Air Conditioning; critical for thermal management

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a raised floor in a data center?
opts: An elevated floor with hollow space beneath for routing power, cooling, and network cables
A reinforced floor to support heavy servers
A floor level for UPS systems
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: A raised floor (12-24 inches high) creates a plenum space beneath used to route power cables, cooling air from CRACs, and network cabling, keeping them accessible and organised.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Raised floor = hollow elevated floor for routing cables and cooling air

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Hot Aisle/Cold Aisle arrangement in data centers?
opts: Separating hot and cold climates in the building
Alternating server rack orientation: cool air drawn from cold aisles (rack fronts), hot exhaust into hot aisles (rack backs), improving cooling efficiency
Placing networking gear near AC units
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Hot/Cold aisle: servers draw cool air from cold aisles (rack fronts) and exhaust hot air into hot aisles (rack backs). Prevents air mixing, improves cooling efficiency by up to 30%.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Hot/Cold aisle = server intake faces cold aisle, exhaust faces hot aisle

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a UPS in a data center context?
opts: Uninterruptible Power Supply providing emergency battery power when mains power fails, allowing graceful shutdown or generator startup
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: UPS (Uninterruptible Power Supply) provides emergency battery power to servers and network equipment when mains power fails, preventing downtime during power outages.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: UPS = battery backup power to prevent downtime during power failures

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is PUE (Power Usage Effectiveness)?
opts: A security metric for data centers
Ratio of total facility power to IT equipment power; closer to 1.0 = more efficient; lower PUE = better
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: PUE = Total Facility Power / IT Equipment Power. PUE of 1.0 = perfect efficiency. Industry average ~1.5, Google achieves ~1.1. Measure of data center energy efficiency.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: PUE = Total Power / IT Power; closer to 1.0 = more efficient data center

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What do TIA-942 Data Center Tiers represent?
opts: Data center reliability levels (Tier I-IV) with increasing redundancy and uptime guarantees
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: TIA-942: Tier I (basic 99.671%), Tier II (redundant components 99.749%), Tier III (concurrent maintainability 99.982%), Tier IV (fault tolerant 99.995%). Higher tier = more redundancy.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: TIA-942 Tiers I-IV: increasing redundancy; Tier IV = 99.995% (fault tolerant)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the Network Operations Center (NOC)?
opts: A centralised facility where IT teams monitor and manage network infrastructure and services 24x7
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: The NOC is the centralised location where IT professionals monitor and manage network infrastructure, servers, and services 24x7, coordinating incident response and escalation.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: NOC = 24x7 centralised network monitoring and management facility

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is data center consolidation?
opts: Reducing physical servers and data centers using virtualisation to improve efficiency and reduce cost, energy use, and complexity
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Consolidation reduces physical infrastructure footprint by combining workloads onto fewer servers (virtualisation), fewer data centers, or fewer storage systems. Goals: cost reduction, efficiency.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: DC Consolidation = fewer physical resources via virtualisation; reduces cost and energy

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is physical security in a data center?
opts: Access controls including guards, biometrics, mantraps, CCTV, and badge readers preventing unauthorised physical access to critical equipment
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Data center physical security: perimeter fencing, security guards, badge access systems, biometric authentication, mantraps (double-door entry), CCTV, and motion sensors.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: DC Physical Security = perimeter + badge/biometric + mantrap + CCTV

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Point of Distribution (PoD) in data center cabling?
opts: An intermediate cabling distribution point connecting main distribution frames to horizontal cabling zones in modular data center design
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: In modular cabling design, a PoD is an intermediate distribution point between the Main Distribution Frame (MDF) and end equipment, improving scalability and reducing cable run lengths.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: PoD = intermediate cabling distribution point in modular data center design

it_infrastructure_devops.q: Which of the following is a Type 1 (bare-metal) hypervisor?
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: VMware ESXi is a Type 1 hypervisor installed directly on server hardware with no underlying OS. Other Type 1: Hyper-V Server, KVM, XenServer. VirtualBox/Workstation = Type 2 (hosted).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Type 1 hypervisors (bare metal): ESXi, Hyper-V Server, KVM, XenServer

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a VM Snapshot?
opts: A backup of the physical server
A saved state of a VM at a specific point in time allowing rollback to that exact state
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: A snapshot captures VM disk, memory, and configuration state at a moment. If something goes wrong (patching, testing), you can revert to that exact state. Not a permanent backup.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Snapshot = point-in-time saved state of VM for quick rollback

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is para-virtualisation?
opts: A technique where the guest OS is modified to use hypercalls to the hypervisor instead of hardware emulation, improving performance
Running multiple OSes without a hypervisor
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Para-virtualisation: guest OS modified to replace privileged hardware calls with hypercalls to the hypervisor, avoiding full emulation and improving performance. Popularised by Xen hypervisor.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Para-virtualisation = modified guest OS uses hypercalls; better performance than full emulation

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is hardware-assisted virtualisation (Intel VT-x / AMD-V)?
opts: CPU extensions allowing hypervisors to run VMs with near-native performance without modifying the guest OS
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Intel VT-x and AMD-V add CPU instructions that let hypervisors efficiently intercept privileged guest OS instructions without binary translation or OS modification, enabling efficient full virtualisation.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: HW-assisted virtualisation = Intel VT-x/AMD-V CPU extensions; near-native full virtualisation

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the difference between a VM Clone and a VM Template?
opts: A clone is a copy of an existing running VM; a template is a master image used to deploy multiple new VMs
A template is a running VM; a clone is stopped
Clone is for production; template is for testing
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: VM Clone: exact runnable copy of an existing VM. Template: golden master image for rapidly provisioning multiple new VMs. Templates cannot run directly - must be deployed as a new VM first.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Clone = copy of VM; Template = master image for provisioning new VMs

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What problem does virtualisation solve in data centers?
opts: Server sprawl and low server utilisation by consolidating multiple workloads onto fewer physical hosts
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Pre-virtualisation servers ran at 10-15% utilisation. Virtualisation consolidates multiple VMs onto fewer physical hosts, improving utilisation to 60-80%, reducing hardware, power, and cooling costs.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Virtualisation solves server sprawl + low utilisation; consolidates workloads

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the difference between SAN and NAS?
opts: SAN is faster; NAS is slower
SAN provides block-level storage over a dedicated network; NAS provides file-level storage over standard Ethernet (NFS/SMB)
SAN is for backup; NAS is for primary storage
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: SAN (block-level): servers see raw storage blocks, ideal for databases and high-performance apps. NAS (file-level): file system shares via NFS/SMB, ideal for file sharing. Different protocol stacks.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: SAN = block-level dedicated network; NAS = file-level shared Ethernet (NFS/SMB)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What protocol does iSCSI use to transport SCSI commands?
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: iSCSI (Internet Small Computer Systems Interface) encapsulates SCSI commands inside TCP/IP packets, enabling block-level SAN storage over standard Ethernet. More affordable than Fibre Channel SAN.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: iSCSI = SCSI over TCP/IP; enables SAN storage on standard Ethernet networks

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is ZFS and what are its key features?
opts: A combined file system and logical volume manager with data integrity checksums, built-in RAID-Z, CoW snapshots, and compression
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: ZFS combines file system + volume management. Features: end-to-end checksums (data integrity), Copy-on-Write snapshots, RAID-Z (software RAID), compression, deduplication. Used in TrueNAS/FreeNAS.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: ZFS = file system + volume manager with checksums, snapshots, RAID-Z, compression

it_infrastructure_devops.q: How does SAN enable High Availability (HA)?
opts: SAN has no role in HA
Shared SAN storage allows multiple hosts to access the same data; if one host fails, another in the cluster takes over without data loss
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Shared SAN storage enables HA clustering: multiple hosts access the same LUNs/volumes. When a host fails, another in the cluster accesses the same storage and continues running applications.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: SAN enables HA: shared storage allows host failover without data loss

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is FreeNAS/TrueNAS?
opts: An open-source NAS/SAN operating system based on FreeBSD+ZFS, supporting iSCSI for SAN and NFS/SMB for NAS
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: FreeNAS (now TrueNAS CORE) is open-source FreeBSD + ZFS storage OS. Supports: NAS (NFS, SMB, AFP) and SAN (iSCSI). Commonly used in labs and small-to-medium businesses.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: FreeNAS/TrueNAS = open-source FreeBSD+ZFS; iSCSI for SAN, NFS/SMB for NAS

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What are the three cloud deployment models?
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Cloud deployment models: Public (shared, provider-managed, multi-tenant), Private (dedicated single-org), Hybrid (combination of public and private connected for workload portability).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Cloud deployment models: Public, Private, Hybrid

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service)?
opts: Providing software applications over the internet
Providing virtualised computing resources (VMs, storage, networks) over the internet; provider manages hardware, you manage OS and above
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: IaaS: on-demand virtual infrastructure. You manage OS and above; provider manages physical hardware and virtualisation. Examples: AWS EC2, Azure VMs, GCP Compute Engine.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: IaaS = virtualised infra; you manage OS+; provider manages hardware

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is PaaS (Platform as a Service)?
opts: A managed development and deployment platform where developers deploy code without managing infrastructure
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: PaaS provides complete development platform: runtime, middleware, databases, deployment tools. Developers upload code without managing VMs or OS. Examples: Heroku, Google App Engine, Azure App Service.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: PaaS = managed dev platform; deploy code without managing infrastructure

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is SaaS (Software as a Service)?
opts: Ready-to-use software delivered over the internet; user manages only data and access, no infrastructure management required
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: SaaS: fully functional software over the internet. No infrastructure, platform, or software management needed by user. Examples: Gmail, Salesforce, Microsoft 365, Slack, Dropbox.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: SaaS = complete ready-to-use software over internet; user manages only data

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is IAM (Identity and Access Management) in cloud?
opts: A framework managing digital identities and controlling access to cloud resources through policies, roles, and permissions
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: IAM controls who (authentication) can do what (authorisation) to which cloud resources. Manages users, groups, roles, and policies. Least Privilege is a key IAM security principle.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: IAM = manage identities and control access to cloud resources via roles/policies

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is cloud elasticity?
opts: The cloud never changes in size
The ability to dynamically scale resources up or down automatically based on demand, paying only for what you use
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Cloud elasticity: auto-scale resources up when demand increases and down when demand decreases. Pay only for what you use. Key cloud advantage over fixed on-premise capacity.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Elasticity = auto scale up/down based on demand; pay only for what you use

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a cloud SLA?
opts: A contract between cloud provider and customer defining guaranteed performance, availability, and remedies if targets are not met
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Cloud SLAs define provider commitments: uptime (e.g., AWS S3 = 99.99%), performance targets, support response times, and service credits if targets are missed.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Cloud SLA = provider contract defining uptime guarantees and remedies for breaches

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What services does AWS provide in the compute category?
opts: EC2 (virtual machines), Lambda (serverless), ECS/EKS (containers), Fargate (serverless containers)
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: AWS compute: EC2 (VMs), Lambda (serverless functions), ECS (container orchestration), EKS (managed Kubernetes), Fargate (serverless containers), Lightsail (simple VPS).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: AWS compute: EC2, Lambda, ECS, EKS, Fargate

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is cloud computing?
opts: On-demand delivery of computing services over the internet with pay-as-you-go pricing; eliminates capital expenditure for hardware
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Cloud computing delivers IT resources on-demand: compute, storage, databases, networking, AI, analytics. Pay-as-you-go pricing. Provider manages physical infrastructure.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Cloud computing = on-demand IT resources over internet; pay-as-you-go model

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the AWS principle of least privilege?
opts: Grant only the minimum IAM permissions required for a specific task, reducing blast radius of compromised credentials
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Least privilege: grant minimum permissions needed. Create policies with specific actions (e.g., s3:GetObject) on specific resources instead of AdministratorAccess. Reduces security risk.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Least Privilege = grant minimum IAM permissions needed; reduces security risk

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Configuration Management in DevOps?
opts: The process of maintaining system integrity and consistency across infrastructure using tools like Chef, Puppet, and Ansible for automated configuration
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Config Management ensures systems are in a known, desired state. Tools (Chef, Puppet, Ansible) automate configuration, replacing manual setup. Key concepts: idempotency, desired state, IaC.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Config Management = automated system state management; Chef/Puppet/Ansible; idempotency

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is idempotency in configuration management?
opts: An operation that produces the same result regardless of how many times it is applied - running it twice has no additional effect
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Idempotency: applying configuration multiple times produces the same result as once. If a package is already installed, the tool skips it without errors. Makes configuration safe to re-run.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Idempotency = same result no matter how many times applied; safe to re-run

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the Chef Workstation?
opts: The developer workstation where cookbook authors write and test recipes using the 'knife' CLI tool to interact with Chef Server
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Chef Workstation: where developers write cookbooks (Ruby DSL), test locally with Test Kitchen, and interact with Chef Server via the knife command-line tool.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Chef Workstation = author cookbooks with knife CLI; test locally with Test Kitchen

it_infrastructure_devops.q: In Chef, what is a Recipe?
opts: A Ruby file containing ordered resource declarations describing the desired configuration state for part of a system
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: A Chef Recipe is a Ruby file listing resources (package, service, file, template) in order. Recipes are organised within Cookbooks and applied to nodes via Run Lists.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Chef Recipe = Ruby file with ordered resource declarations; part of a Cookbook

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Puppet's key language characteristic?
opts: Puppet uses a declarative DSL in .pp manifests - you declare WHAT state you want, not HOW to achieve it
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Puppet uses its own declarative DSL in .pp manifest files. You describe desired state (resource type + attributes), and Puppet determines HOW to achieve it on each target OS.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Puppet = declarative DSL (.pp manifests); declare WHAT state, not HOW to achieve it

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the key difference between Chef and Puppet languages?
opts: Chef uses Ruby DSL (imperative); Puppet uses its own declarative DSL (.pp manifests); Ansible uses YAML
Chef uses YAML; Puppet uses Python
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Chef: Ruby DSL, more flexible, requires Ruby knowledge, imperative. Puppet: own declarative DSL, describe WHAT you want, Puppet determines HOW. Ansible: YAML playbooks (simplest, most readable).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Chef = Ruby DSL (imperative); Puppet = own DSL (declarative); Ansible = YAML

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is 'desired state configuration' in Chef/Puppet?
opts: The principle that tools define the target state of a system and automatically detect and correct drift from that state
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Desired State: declare HOW the system should look, and the tool continuously ensures the system matches that state. If drift occurs (manual changes), the tool automatically corrects it.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Desired State = declare target state; tool auto-corrects any drift

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the Chef architecture model?
opts: Pull-based: Workstation (authors cookbooks), Chef Server (stores/distributes), Chef Client/Nodes (pull and apply cookbooks)
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Chef pull-based architecture: Workstation (write cookbooks with knife), Chef Server (central policy hub), Nodes/Clients (pull catalog and apply). chef-client runs on each managed node.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Chef = pull-based: Workstation > Server > Node/Client (pull+apply)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the Prometheus data model?
opts: Time-series data identified by metric name and key-value labels; format: metric_name{labels} value timestamp
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Prometheus stores data as time-series: streams of timestamped values with same metric name and label set. Labels enable multi-dimensional querying and aggregation via PromQL.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Prometheus model = time-series: metric_name{labels} value timestamp

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What does Node Exporter collect for Prometheus?
opts: Linux/Unix hardware and OS metrics: CPU usage, memory, disk I/O, filesystem, network statistics, and load average
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Node Exporter is the standard Prometheus exporter for Linux/Unix system metrics: CPU, memory, disk I/O, filesystem usage, network statistics, and system load average.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Node Exporter = Linux system metrics exporter (CPU/memory/disk/network)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is AlertManager in the Prometheus ecosystem?
opts: A component handling alerts from Prometheus: deduplication, grouping, routing, and sending notifications via email/Slack/PagerDuty
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: AlertManager receives alert firings from Prometheus, handles: deduplication (same alert from multiple sources), grouping (related alerts), silencing, inhibition, routing to receivers.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: AlertManager = handles Prometheus alerts: deduplicate + group + route to receivers

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Grafana used for in a Prometheus stack?
opts: Open-source data visualisation platform for creating dashboards and graphs from Prometheus metrics via PromQL queries
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Grafana queries Prometheus via PromQL and creates rich dashboards with graphs, gauges, heatmaps, and alerts. Standard monitoring stack: Prometheus (metrics) + Grafana (visualisation).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Grafana + Prometheus = standard monitoring stack; Grafana visualises Prometheus metrics

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the Prometheus scrape interval?
opts: How often data is stored to disk
How often Prometheus polls target /metrics endpoints to collect metrics; configurable, default is 15 seconds
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: scrape_interval defines how often Prometheus pulls from each target's /metrics endpoint. Default 15s globally, overridable per-job. More frequent = finer granularity but more storage.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Prometheus scrape_interval = how often metrics are pulled from targets; default 15s

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Dockerfile?
opts: A Docker configuration file for networking
A text file with build instructions (FROM, RUN, COPY, CMD, EXPOSE, ENV) that Docker reads to build a custom image layer by layer
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: A Dockerfile: FROM (base image), RUN (execute commands, creates layers), COPY (add files from build context), CMD (default run command), EXPOSE (document port), ENV (environment vars).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Dockerfile = text file with image build instructions; FROM+RUN+COPY+CMD

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Docker Compose?
opts: A tool defining and running multi-container Docker applications using a docker-compose.yml YAML file; docker compose up/down
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Docker Compose: docker-compose.yml defines services (containers), networks, and volumes. docker compose up -d (start all in background), docker compose down (stop and remove), docker compose ps (status).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Docker Compose = multi-container orchestration via docker-compose.yml; up/down

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Docker Volume?
opts: A persistent storage mechanism managed by Docker allowing data to persist beyond container lifecycle and be shared between containers
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Docker Volumes: managed by Docker in /var/lib/docker/volumes. Persist when containers are removed. Can be shared between containers. Preferred over bind mounts for data portability in production.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Docker Volume = managed persistent storage; survives container removal; shareable

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Docker Hub?
opts: The official public Docker image registry for pushing and pulling container images; provides official images (nginx, ubuntu, mysql)
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Docker Hub is Docker's official cloud registry. Push custom images (docker push), pull official images (docker pull). Commands: docker push, docker pull, docker tag.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Docker Hub = official public Docker image registry; push/pull images

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What does 'docker run -d -p 8080:80 nginx' do?
opts: Starts an nginx container in background (-d) mode, mapping host port 8080 to container port 80; access via http://localhost:8080
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: docker run: create+start; -d: detached (background); -p 8080:80: host port 8080 maps to container port 80; nginx: official nginx image. Access via http://localhost:8080.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: docker run -d -p host:container image = background container with port mapping

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What are the main Docker network types?
opts: Docker uses only one network type
Bridge (default, same-host containers), host (shares host network), overlay (multi-host/Swarm), none (no networking)
Docker networking is only for Kubernetes
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Docker networks: Bridge (default - isolated network, containers communicate by name), Host (shares host network stack), Overlay (Swarm multi-host), None (no network access).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Docker networks: bridge (default), host, overlay (Swarm multi-host), none

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Docker layer caching?
opts: Docker reuses previously built image layers that haven't changed, significantly speeding up subsequent builds
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Each Dockerfile instruction creates a cached layer. Unchanged instructions reuse cached layers. Optimise: put frequently changing instructions (COPY source code) after stable ones (RUN apt-get install).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Docker layer caching = reuse unchanged layers; put stable instructions early

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a multi-stage Docker build?
opts: A Dockerfile technique using multiple FROM statements where only required artifacts are copied to the final slim image, discarding build tools
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Multi-stage: Stage 1 = full SDK (compile code). Stage 2 = minimal runtime - COPY --from=stage1 compiled binary. Final image has no build tools. Smaller, more secure production image.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Multi-stage build = build in fat image, COPY binary to slim runtime; smaller+safer

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the ENTRYPOINT instruction in a Dockerfile?
opts: Sets the fixed executable that always runs when a container starts; unlike CMD, it cannot be easily overridden by docker run arguments
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: ENTRYPOINT sets the fixed container executable. CMD provides default arguments to ENTRYPOINT. ENTRYPOINT can only be overridden with --entrypoint flag. Use together: ENTRYPOINT+CMD pattern.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: ENTRYPOINT = fixed container executable; CMD provides default args

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is .dockerignore?
opts: A file telling Docker which files to exclude from the build context, speeding up builds and preventing sensitive files from being included in images
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: .dockerignore excludes: node_modules/, .git/, *.log, .env. Reduces build context size sent to Docker daemon, speeds builds, prevents secrets accidentally ending up in images.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: dockerignore = exclude files from build context; faster builds + prevent secret leaks

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is container image tagging?
opts: Adding a label to a running container
Versioning Docker images using image_name:tag format (e.g., myapp:1.0, myapp:latest) for tracking versions and enabling rollbacks
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Image tags version Docker images. Tag: docker tag myapp:latest myapp:1.2.3. Push: docker push username/myapp:1.2.3. Avoid using only 'latest' in production - use semantic versioning.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Image tags = versions (image:tag); semantic versioning; avoid latest-only in production

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What does 'docker ps -a' show?
opts: All containers including stopped ones with CONTAINER ID, IMAGE, STATUS, PORTS, and NAME
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: docker ps: only running containers. docker ps -a: all containers including stopped/exited ones. Output: CONTAINER ID, IMAGE, COMMAND, CREATED, STATUS, PORTS, NAMES.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: docker ps = running containers; docker ps -a = all including stopped

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the purpose of environment variables in Docker?
opts: To set the container hostname only
To pass configuration values (API keys, database URLs, ports) into containers at runtime without hardcoding them in images
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Docker env vars inject config at runtime: -e VAR=value or env: in docker-compose.yml. Follows 12-factor methodology - separate config from code. Never hardcode secrets in Dockerfile.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Docker env vars = inject config at runtime; -e flag or env: in Compose; no hardcoded secrets

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What does 'git clone' do?
opts: Creates a complete local copy of a remote repository including all files, branches, tags, and commit history; automatically sets remote as 'origin'
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: git clone <URL> downloads the complete repository. All history, branches, and tags included. Remote automatically named 'origin'. Foundation of distributed Git workflows.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: git clone = download full remote repo (all files + history); remote named 'origin'

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the difference between 'git fetch' and 'git pull'?
opts: git fetch downloads changes from remote without merging; git pull = git fetch + git merge (auto-merges into current branch)
git pull downloads; git fetch merges
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: git fetch: safely downloads to FETCH_HEAD without touching working files. git pull: fetch + merge into current branch. Review fetched changes before merging - safer workflow.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: git fetch = download only; git pull = fetch + merge; fetch safer for review first

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What does 'git commit' do?
opts: Records staged changes to local repository history as a new commit with SHA-1 hash and descriptive message - does NOT push to remote
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: git commit records staged changes (after git add) in local history with SHA-1 hash and message. Local only - use git push to share with remote repository.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: git commit = save staged snapshot to LOCAL repo; doesn't push to remote

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Pull Request (PR) in GitHub?
opts: A mechanism to propose merging changes from a feature branch into a target branch; enables code review by team members before merging
A way to pull Docker images
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: A PR on GitHub proposes merging a feature branch. Team reviews code, leaves comments, requests changes, approves. Integrates with CI/CD for automated checks. Called Merge Request in GitLab.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Pull Request = propose branch merge; enables code review before merging

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What does 'git stash' do?
opts: Temporarily saves uncommitted changes to a stack, cleaning the working directory; restore with git stash pop
Commits changes to a hidden branch
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: git stash: temporarily shelve uncommitted changes. Commands: git stash (save), git stash list (view stack), git stash pop (restore+remove), git stash apply (restore without removing).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: git stash = temporarily save uncommitted work; stash/pop for context-switching

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is 'git cherry-pick'?
opts: A way to select best commits for review
A command applying a specific commit from one branch to the current branch without merging the entire source branch
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: git cherry-pick <commit-hash> applies a specific commit to current branch. Creates a new commit with same changes but different hash. Useful for: porting bug fixes without full branch merge.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: git cherry-pick = apply specific commit to current branch without full merge

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is GitHub Actions?
opts: GitHub's built-in CI/CD automation platform using YAML workflows in .github/workflows/ triggered on events like push, pull_request, or schedule
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: GitHub Actions: YAML workflows in .github/workflows/*.yml triggered by GitHub events (push, PR, schedule, workflow_dispatch). Jobs with steps using marketplace Actions or custom scripts.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: GitHub Actions = GitHub-native CI/CD; YAML workflows in .github/workflows/

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is .gitignore?
opts: A text file specifying files and directories Git should not track (build artifacts, node_modules, secrets, IDE files)
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: .gitignore lists patterns Git should not track. Common: node_modules/, *.log, .env, build/, __pycache__/, .DS_Store. Prevents committing large binaries, generated files, or sensitive credentials.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: gitignore = tells Git which files not to track (builds, secrets, IDE files)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What are the main stages in a typical Jenkins CI/CD pipeline?
opts: Checkout, Build, Test, Package, Deploy, (optionally Notify)
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Jenkins pipeline stages: Checkout (git pull from SCM), Build (compile), Test (unit/integration/quality), Package (artifact or Docker image), Deploy (to environment), Notify (Slack/email).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Jenkins pipeline: Checkout > Build > Test > Package > Deploy > Notify

it_infrastructure_devops.q: How does Jenkins integrate with GitHub?
opts: Jenkins uses webhooks (GitHub notifies Jenkins on push/PR) or polling to automatically trigger pipeline builds on code changes
Jenkins clones GitHub repos manually only
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Jenkins-GitHub integration: GitHub webhook pushes event to Jenkins on push/PR, triggering build immediately (preferred). Alternatively, Jenkins polls GitHub at intervals (less efficient, more load).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Jenkins + GitHub: webhook triggers build on push/PR (preferred) or polling

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the Jenkins Master-Agent architecture?
opts: Jenkins Master coordinates builds, UI, and scheduling; Agents (workers) execute actual build steps on remote machines, enabling distributed parallel builds
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Jenkins Master: orchestration, UI, job scheduling. Agents: remote machines executing build steps. Agents labelled for specific tasks (docker-agent, linux, windows). Enables parallel builds.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Jenkins Master = orchestrator; Agents = workers executing build steps on remote machines

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Jenkins Plugin?
opts: An extension adding new functionality to Jenkins; examples: Git, Docker Pipeline, Kubernetes, Slack Notification, JUnit, Blue Ocean
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Jenkins 1800+ plugins extend functionality: Git (SCM), Docker Pipeline (build images), Kubernetes (cloud agents), Slack (notifications), JUnit (test reports), Pipeline (Jenkinsfile support).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Jenkins plugins = extensions; 1800+ available; Git/Docker/Kubernetes/Slack/JUnit

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Amazon EC2?
opts: Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud providing scalable, resizable virtual machine instances billed per second
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: EC2 (Elastic Compute Cloud): AWS's core IaaS service providing resizable VMs. Choose: instance type (CPU/RAM), AMI (OS+software template), storage (EBS), networking (VPC/Security Group).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: EC2 = AWS virtual machines; choose instance type + AMI; billed per-second

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is an AMI (Amazon Machine Image)?
opts: A pre-configured template containing OS, software, and configuration used to launch EC2 instances
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: AMI is a template for EC2 instances: OS, installed software, configurations, data volumes. Public AMIs: Amazon Linux, Ubuntu, Windows Server. Create custom AMIs from running instances.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: AMI = EC2 instance template (OS + software + config); launch instances from it

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Amazon S3 and what is its durability?
opts: Simple Storage Service providing virtually unlimited object storage with 99.999999999% (11 nines) durability
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: S3 is AWS's object storage: objects stored in buckets, accessible via HTTPS. 11 nines durability. Use cases: backup, static website hosting, data lake, log storage, CDN origin.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: S3 = AWS object storage; 11 nines durability; buckets + objects; HTTPS access

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) in AWS?
opts: A logically isolated virtual network in AWS where you define IP ranges, subnets, routing tables, internet gateways, and security groups for your resources
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: VPC is a logically isolated section of AWS cloud. You define: CIDR IP range, subnets (public/private), route tables, internet gateway, NAT gateway, security groups. Isolated from other customers.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: VPC = isolated virtual network in AWS; define subnets/routing/security

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the difference between Public and Private Subnets in AWS VPC?
opts: Public subnet has a route to Internet Gateway (internet-accessible, instances can have public IPs); Private subnet has no direct internet route (uses NAT Gateway for outbound-only access)
Public is free; private is paid
Public for databases; private for web servers
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Public subnet: IGW route (0.0.0.0/0 > IGW), instances can have public IPs, directly reachable from internet. Private subnet: no IGW, outbound internet via NAT Gateway, not directly reachable.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Public subnet = internet-accessible via IGW; Private = no IGW, NAT for outbound only

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What are the three core AWS services commonly used in DevOps labs?
opts: EC2 (virtual machines), S3 (object storage), Lambda (serverless functions)
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Core AWS DevOps services: EC2 (run VMs/web servers), S3 (store artifacts/logs/static files), Lambda (serverless automation). Foundational for CI/CD, IaC, and monitoring on AWS.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Core AWS DevOps: EC2 (VMs) + S3 (storage) + Lambda (serverless)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is AWS ECR (Elastic Container Registry)?
opts: AWS's managed private Docker container image registry integrated with ECS/EKS/IAM, with image vulnerability scanning and lifecycle policies
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: ECR is AWS private Docker registry. Integrates with ECS/EKS (native image pulling), IAM (access control). Features: image scanning for CVEs (Trivy-based), lifecycle policies (auto-delete old images).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: ECR = AWS private Docker registry; ECS/EKS/IAM integration; image scanning

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is AWS CloudWatch?
opts: AWS's monitoring and observability service collecting metrics, logs, and events from AWS services with alarms, dashboards, and automated actions
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: CloudWatch: collect metrics (EC2 CPU, RDS, Lambda invocations), store logs (CloudWatch Logs), create alarms (trigger SNS/Auto Scaling/Lambda), build dashboards. Needs agent for OS-level metrics.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: CloudWatch = AWS native monitoring; metrics + logs + alarms + dashboards

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Kubernetes Pod?
opts: The smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes: one or more containers sharing the same network namespace (IP) and storage volumes
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: A Pod is K8s's atomic unit. Containers share: network (same IP/ports) and storage (volumes). Pods are ephemeral - controllers (Deployment, StatefulSet) recreate failed pods automatically.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Pod = smallest K8s unit; containers share network + storage; ephemeral

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Kubernetes Service?
opts: A K8s abstraction exposing a set of Pods as a stable network endpoint with a ClusterIP, DNS name, and load balancing
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: K8s Service provides stable network endpoint for Pod set selected via labels. Types: ClusterIP (internal cluster access), NodePort (exposed on each node), LoadBalancer (cloud LB), ExternalName.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: K8s Service = stable endpoint for Pods; ClusterIP/NodePort/LoadBalancer types

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What components make up the Kubernetes Control Plane?
opts: API Server (communication hub), etcd (state database), Scheduler (pod placement), Controller Manager (desired state enforcement)
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Control Plane: API Server (all communication goes through here), etcd (cluster state database), Scheduler (assigns pods to nodes), Controller Manager (reconciles desired vs actual state).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: K8s Control Plane: API Server + etcd + Scheduler + Controller Manager

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is etcd in Kubernetes?
opts: A distributed key-value store serving as Kubernetes' primary database for all cluster state (pods, services, configs, secrets)
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: etcd is a highly available distributed key-value store. All K8s state stored here. Backing up etcd is critical for disaster recovery. Loss of etcd = loss of entire cluster configuration.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: etcd = K8s cluster state database (distributed key-value); back up regularly

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Docker Swarm mode?
opts: Docker's native container orchestration for managing a cluster of Docker hosts with built-in service scaling and load balancing; simpler than Kubernetes
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Docker Swarm mode: init with docker swarm init (first manager), workers join with docker swarm join --token. Services: docker service create --replicas N image. Manager handles orchestration.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Docker Swarm = Docker's native orchestration; Manager + Workers; simpler than K8s

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Kubernetes ReplicaSet?
opts: A K8s resource ensuring a specified number of identical Pod replicas are running at all times, automatically replacing failed pods
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: ReplicaSet maintains stable set of pod replicas. If a Pod fails, ReplicaSet creates a new one immediately. Usually managed by Deployments (don't create ReplicaSets directly).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: ReplicaSet = maintains desired number of Pod replicas; auto-replaces failed pods

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Kubernetes DaemonSet?
opts: A K8s resource ensuring one copy of a Pod runs on every (or selected) node; used for node-level agents like log collectors and monitoring exporters
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: DaemonSet: one pod per node. Common: log collection (Fluentd), monitoring (Node Exporter, Datadog agent), networking (Calico, Weave, Cilium). New nodes automatically get the pod.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: DaemonSet = one pod per node; node-level agents (log/monitoring/network)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Kubernetes StatefulSet?
opts: A K8s workload for stateful applications providing stable pod identities, ordered deployment, and persistent storage (PVC) per pod
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: StatefulSet: ordered pod creation/deletion, stable pod names (web-0, web-1, web-2), stable DNS names, dedicated PersistentVolumeClaims per pod. For MySQL, Cassandra, Kafka, ZooKeeper, Redis Sentinel.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: StatefulSet = ordered stable-named pods + dedicated PVCs; for stateful apps (DBs)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Kubernetes RBAC?
opts: Role-Based Access Control using Roles, ClusterRoles, RoleBindings, and ClusterRoleBindings to control who can perform what actions on which K8s resources
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: K8s RBAC: Role (namespaced permissions), ClusterRole (cluster-wide), RoleBinding (bind Role to user/SA), ClusterRoleBinding. Least-privilege. Restrict pod service account permissions.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: K8s RBAC = Roles + Bindings; least-privilege access to K8s API resources

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA)?
opts: A K8s resource automatically adjusting pod replica count based on CPU/memory utilisation or custom metrics
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: HPA auto-adjusts pod replicas based on metrics. Default: CPU utilisation (e.g., target 50% = scale up when pods average >50% CPU). Supports custom metrics. Requires metrics-server.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: HPA = auto-scale pod replicas based on CPU/custom metrics; requires metrics-server

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is kubectl apply vs kubectl create?
opts: kubectl create fails if resource exists (imperative); kubectl apply creates or updates idempotently (declarative) - preferred for CI/CD and GitOps workflows
kubectl apply is for deployments only
kubectl create is for services only
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: kubectl create: imperative, fails if resource exists. kubectl apply -f: declarative, creates if new or updates if changed. Idempotent - safe to run repeatedly. Use apply in GitOps pipelines.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: kubectl apply = idempotent create/update; kubectl create = fails if exists; prefer apply

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a PersistentVolume (PV) and PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC)?
opts: PV = cluster-wide storage resource (admin provisioned or dynamic via StorageClass); PVC = pod's storage request; K8s binds PVC to matching PV
PV for databases; PVC for applications
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: PV: storage provisioned by admin or dynamically via StorageClass. PVC: pod requests specific storage (size, access mode). K8s binds PVC to matching PV. Data persists across pod restarts/rescheduling.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: PV = storage resource; PVC = pod storage request; binding decouples storage from pod lifecycle

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Kubernetes CronJob?
opts: A K8s resource creating Jobs on a cron-based schedule (e.g., '0 2 * * *' for 2am daily) for backups, reports, and periodic cleanup tasks
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: CronJob creates Jobs on a cron schedule. Manages history (successfulJobsHistoryLimit, failedJobsHistoryLimit). Use for: DB backups, report generation, cache warming, scheduled batch processing.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: CronJob = scheduled K8s Job using cron expression; backups/reports/batch on schedule

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Kubernetes init container?
opts: The first pod in a deployment
A specialised container that must run to completion before app containers start; used for setup tasks like waiting for dependencies or running migrations
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Init containers run sequentially before app containers. Each must complete. Uses: wait for service (until curl db:5432), populate config files, run DB schema migrations, clone git repo.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Init container = runs to completion before app container; setup/wait-for-deps pattern

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Kubernetes ConfigMap?
opts: A K8s resource storing non-sensitive configuration data (key-value pairs) injected into pods as environment variables or mounted as configuration files
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: ConfigMap decouples config from images. Store: app settings, URLs, feature flags, config files. Consumed as: env vars (envFrom/env valueFrom), or volume mounts (config files). Use Secrets for sensitive data.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: ConfigMap = non-sensitive config data for pods; env vars or mounted files

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Kubernetes Secret?
opts: A K8s resource for sensitive data (passwords, tokens, TLS certs) stored base64-encoded; restrict with RBAC and enable encryption at rest for production security
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Secrets: sensitive data base64-encoded (not encrypted by default - enable encryption at rest in etcd). Types: Opaque, tls, docker-registry. Use RBAC to restrict access. Never store secrets in ConfigMaps.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: K8s Secret = sensitive data base64-encoded; enable encryption at rest + RBAC restriction

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is an Ansible Module?
opts: A reusable unit of code executing a specific task on managed hosts: apt, yum, copy, service, file, template, user, git, uri, etc.
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Ansible modules: apt/yum/dnf (packages), copy/template/file (files), service/systemd (services), user/group (users), command/shell (run commands), uri (HTTP requests). Idempotent.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Ansible Module = reusable task unit; apt/yum/copy/service/file/template etc.

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Ansible Vault?
opts: An Ansible feature encrypting sensitive data (passwords, API keys) using AES256 encryption, making it safe to commit to version control
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Ansible Vault encrypts sensitive variables/files with AES256. Commands: ansible-vault create, edit, encrypt, decrypt, view. Encrypted files can be safely committed to Git repos.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Ansible Vault = AES256 encrypt secrets; safe for Git commit; ansible-vault create/edit/encrypt

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What does ansible-galaxy do?
opts: A CLI tool for finding, installing, and managing Ansible roles and collections from the Ansible Galaxy public registry (galaxy.ansible.com)
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: ansible-galaxy: install geerlingguy.nginx (install role), ansible-galaxy init my-role (create role skeleton), ansible-galaxy collection install community.general (install collection).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: ansible-galaxy = install/manage roles and collections; galaxy.ansible.com marketplace

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is an ad-hoc command in Ansible?
opts: A one-liner Ansible command running a single module against inventory without writing a playbook; useful for quick tasks and diagnostics
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Ad-hoc: ansible all -m ping (test connectivity), ansible webservers -m service -a 'name=nginx state=restarted' (restart nginx). Quick one-off tasks without creating a full playbook.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Ansible ad-hoc = one-liner; quick tasks without playbook; ansible all -m ping

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the ansible.cfg file?
opts: Ansible's configuration file defining defaults: inventory path, remote user, SSH settings, roles path, and other settings
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: ansible.cfg configures Ansible: [defaults] inventory = ./hosts, remote_user = ubuntu, private_key_file = ~/.ssh/id_rsa, host_key_checking = False. Searched in current directory first.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: ansible.cfg = Ansible configuration file; defaults for inventory, SSH, roles

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is HCL (HashiCorp Configuration Language)?
opts: The declarative configuration language used in Terraform .tf files to define infrastructure resources, variables, and outputs
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: HCL is Terraform's config language: human-friendly, machine-parseable. Supports variables, outputs, data sources, loops (count, for_each), conditionals. Files use .tf extension.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: HCL = Terraform's declarative config language in .tf files

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the Terraform workflow?
opts: terraform init (setup) > terraform plan (dry run) > terraform apply (provision) > terraform destroy (teardown)
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Terraform workflow: init (download providers/modules), plan (show changes, no modifications), apply (provision infrastructure), destroy (delete all resources). Always run plan before apply.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Terraform: init > plan (dry run) > apply (provision) > destroy

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Terraform State?
opts: A file (terraform.tfstate) mapping configuration resources to real-world infrastructure; enables Terraform to calculate diffs for subsequent applies
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Terraform state records what currently exists. Enables calculating diffs. For teams: remote state in S3 (or Terraform Cloud) with DynamoDB locking prevents concurrent modification conflicts.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Terraform state = maps config to real resources; remote state (S3+DynamoDB) for teams

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Remote State in Terraform?
opts: Storing terraform.tfstate in a remote backend (AWS S3 with DynamoDB locking) enabling safe team collaboration and preventing concurrent state corruption
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Remote state: tfstate in S3 (or Terraform Cloud, Azure Blob, GCS). DynamoDB provides state locking. Prevents concurrent applies corrupting state. Enables team collaboration. Essential for teams.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Remote State = shared tfstate in S3+DynamoDB lock; prevents team conflicts

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What does 'terraform destroy' do?
opts: Rolling back the last change only
Terminates and removes all resources defined in the Terraform configuration; shows destruction plan before executing
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: terraform destroy shows destruction plan then removes all managed resources. Irreversible for many resources. Use with extreme caution in production. Confirm by typing 'yes'.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: terraform destroy = remove ALL Terraform-managed resources; use with extreme caution

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is 'terraform import'?
opts: Brings existing infrastructure not created by Terraform under Terraform management by adding it to the state file
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: terraform import reads existing cloud resource (e.g., manually created EC2 instance) and adds it to Terraform state. After importing, write matching HCL config. Enables gradual Terraform adoption.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: terraform import = adopt existing resources into Terraform; write HCL config after

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Terraform data source?
opts: A way to query existing resources or APIs read-only within a Terraform config without managing them; referenced as data.type.name.attribute
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Data sources read existing resources: data 'aws_ami' 'ubuntu' { most_recent = true }. Reference: ami = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id. Doesn't create/manage - only reads for use in config.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Terraform data source = read existing resources/APIs; reference with data.type.name.attr

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is 'terraform validate'?
opts: Checks Terraform configuration syntax and internal consistency without making API calls; fast error detection before plan/apply
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: terraform validate checks syntax and internal consistency (valid resource types, required attributes, correct references). No API calls. Fast check before plan. Part of CI/CD validation step.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: terraform validate = syntax + consistency check; no API calls; catch errors early

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Terraform output block?
opts: Declares values to expose after apply (e.g., instance IP, bucket name); useful for cross-module data sharing and displaying important resource information
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: output 'instance_ip' { value = aws_instance.web.public_ip } displayed after apply. Share between modules, expose important resource attributes, chain Terraform configurations.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Terraform output = expose values after apply; share between modules; show key resource info

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is terraform.tfvars?
opts: A file providing variable values to Terraform configurations, avoiding hardcoding values in .tf files and enabling environment-specific configurations
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: terraform.tfvars provides input variable values, overriding defaults. Environment-specific: dev.tfvars, prod.tfvars. Specify with -var-file=prod.tfvars. Don't commit sensitive values to Git.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: terraform.tfvars = variable values file; environment-specific configs; don't commit secrets

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is 'terraform fmt'?
opts: A command automatically formatting Terraform .tf files to canonical style and consistent indentation for code consistency
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: terraform fmt auto-formats all .tf files to Terraform's canonical style (consistent indentation, alignment). Run before committing. Use -recursive for subdirectories. Part of pre-commit hooks.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: terraform fmt = auto-format .tf files to canonical style; run before commit

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Infrastructure as Code (IaC)?
opts: Managing and provisioning infrastructure through machine-readable configuration files, enabling repeatability, version control, and automated provisioning
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: IaC: infrastructure defined as code files, version-controlled (Git), testable, repeatable. Benefits: consistency, auditability, fast provisioning, no drift. Tools: Terraform, CloudFormation, Pulumi, Ansible.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: IaC = define infrastructure as code; version control, automate, repeat reliably

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the difference between Continuous Delivery and Continuous Deployment?
opts: Continuous Delivery: code is always deployable, manual production approval; Continuous Deployment: fully automated, every passing build auto-deploys to production
Delivery is automated; Deployment is manual
Delivery for dev; Deployment for prod
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Continuous Delivery: built, tested, staged automatically; human approves production push. Continuous Deployment: no manual gate - every passing build deploys to production automatically.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Delivery = manual gate to prod; Deployment = fully automated to prod

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a container registry?
opts: A repository for storing, versioning, and distributing Docker container images; examples: Docker Hub, Amazon ECR, Azure ACR, GitHub Container Registry, Harbor
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Container registries: push images after building, pull to deploy. Public: Docker Hub. Private: Amazon ECR, Azure ACR, GCP Artifact Registry, Harbor (self-hosted). Integrate with CI/CD pipelines.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Container registry = image repository; push after build, pull to deploy

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the difference between vertical and horizontal scaling?
opts: Vertical scaling adds more CPU/RAM to existing server (scale-up); Horizontal scaling adds more server instances behind a load balancer (scale-out)
Vertical is for storage; horizontal is for compute
Vertical for cloud; horizontal for on-premise
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Vertical (scale-up): bigger VM - more CPU/RAM. Has limits, often needs downtime. Horizontal (scale-out): more instances + load balancer. Cloud-native preferred - enables elasticity and HA.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Vertical = bigger server (scale-up); Horizontal = more servers (scale-out); prefer horizontal

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a load balancer?
opts: A device or service distributing incoming network traffic across multiple backend servers to ensure no single server is overwhelmed, improving availability and performance
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Load balancer distributes traffic via algorithms: round-robin, least connections, IP hash. Layer 4 (TCP/UDP) or Layer 7 (HTTP - content-based routing). Examples: NGINX, HAProxy, AWS ELB.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Load balancer = distributes traffic across servers; improves availability and performance

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the difference between a container and a virtual machine?
opts: Containers share the host OS kernel (lightweight, fast startup, MBs in size); VMs have a full OS per instance (heavier, slower start, GBs in size)
VMs share the kernel; containers have full OS
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: VMs: full OS + kernel, hardware virtualisation (hypervisor), minutes to start, GBs in size, more isolated. Containers: shared kernel, OS-level virtualisation (namespaces/cgroups), seconds to start, MBs.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Container = shared kernel (lightweight, fast, MBs); VM = full OS (heavier, more isolated, GBs)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is observability in DevOps?
opts: The ability to understand a system's internal state from its external outputs; three pillars: Metrics (Prometheus), Logs (ELK/Loki), and Traces (Jaeger)
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Observability: understand internals from outputs. Metrics (quantitative measurements), Logs (event records), Traces (request journeys through microservices). Essential for diagnosing distributed systems.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Observability = Metrics (Prometheus) + Logs (ELK/Loki) + Traces (Jaeger)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is MTTR in DevOps?
opts: Mean Time To Recovery - the average time to restore service after a failure; a key reliability and SRE metric
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: MTTR (Mean Time To Recovery): average time from failure detection to service restoration. Lower MTTR = better reliability. DevOps practices (monitoring, runbooks, automation) minimise MTTR.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: MTTR = average time to restore after failure; lower = better; key reliability KPI

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is blue-green deployment?
opts: A zero-downtime deployment strategy with two identical environments (blue=current live, green=new version); switch load balancer from blue to green; instant rollback by switching back
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Blue-green: two identical production environments. Blue = current live. Green = new version. Switch LB from blue to green after testing. Instant rollback = switch back to blue. Doubles infra cost temporarily.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Blue-green = two envs; switch LB from blue to green; instant rollback; zero downtime

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is canary deployment?
opts: A deployment strategy releasing new version to a small percentage of users first, monitoring for issues before gradually rolling out to all users
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Canary: route ~5% of traffic to new version, monitor metrics/errors. Gradually increase to 100% if healthy, roll back if problems detected. Lower risk than full deployment. Early warning system.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Canary = small % to new version first; monitor; gradually increase; lower risk rollout

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a webhook?
opts: An HTTP callback automatically triggered by an event allowing one system to notify another in real-time; enables event-driven CI/CD (GitHub notifying Jenkins on push)
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Webhook: HTTP POST sent by source (GitHub) to destination URL (Jenkins/Slack) on specific event (push, PR, release). Real-time event-driven integration without polling. Core CI/CD triggering mechanism.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Webhook = HTTP callback on event; enables real-time event-driven CI/CD

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is DevSecOps?
opts: The integration of security practices throughout the DevOps pipeline - shift security left by automating security testing in CI/CD rather than only at the end
A security team working after DevOps
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: DevSecOps: embed security in every SDLC stage. Code: SAST (SonarQube, Semgrep), secrets scanning. Build: dependency scanning (Snyk, OWASP). Deploy: container scanning (Trivy). Run: WAF, runtime security.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: DevSecOps = security in every SDLC stage; SAST+dependency+container scanning in CI/CD

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is SonarQube in DevOps?
opts: A code quality and security analysis platform (SAST) detecting bugs, code smells, security vulnerabilities, and coverage gaps; integrates with CI/CD via quality gates
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: SonarQube: SAST tool detecting bugs, code smells, security vulnerabilities (SQL injection, XSS), duplications, measures test coverage. Quality gates can fail CI builds on threshold violations.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: SonarQube = SAST; detect bugs/security/smells; quality gates fail builds on violations

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Agile methodology?
opts: An iterative development approach emphasising flexibility, customer collaboration, and delivering working software in short cycles based on the Agile Manifesto
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Agile: iterative development based on Agile Manifesto (2001). Values: individuals over processes, working software over documentation, customer collaboration over contracts, responding to change.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Agile = iterative, flexible development; short cycles; customer collaboration

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Scrum?
opts: An Agile framework using time-boxed Sprints (1-4 weeks) with Product Owner, Scrum Master, and Dev Team roles and four core ceremonies
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Scrum: Sprints (1-4 weeks). Roles: Product Owner (backlog), Scrum Master (process facilitator), Dev Team. Ceremonies: Sprint Planning, Daily Scrum, Sprint Review, Retrospective.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Scrum = Agile framework; Sprints + PO/SM/Dev Team + 4 ceremonies

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Kanban in DevOps?
opts: A visual workflow management method using a board with columns and WIP (Work In Progress) limits to visualise and optimise continuous flow
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Kanban: visual board showing work items moving through stages. WIP limits prevent bottlenecks. No fixed sprints - continuous flow. Originates from Toyota's lean manufacturing. Popular in operations/support.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Kanban = visual board with WIP limits; continuous flow; no fixed sprints

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is an API Gateway?
opts: A single entry point for client requests to backend microservices handling routing, authentication, rate limiting, SSL termination, and monitoring
A type of load balancer only
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: API Gateway: single client entry point routing to microservices. Handles: auth/authorisation, rate limiting, SSL termination, request/response transformation, caching, monitoring. Examples: AWS API GW, Kong, NGINX.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: API Gateway = single entry for microservices; routing + auth + rate limit + SSL + monitoring

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What are Linux namespaces in the context of containers?
opts: Linux kernel features isolating system resources (PID, network, IPC, UTS, mount, user namespaces) for each container ensuring process isolation
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Linux namespaces: PID (process IDs), Network (interfaces/routes), Mount (filesystem), IPC (inter-process comm), UTS (hostname/domain), User (UIDs/GIDs). Docker uses namespaces to isolate containers.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Linux namespaces = resource isolation for containers (PID/Net/Mount/IPC/UTS/User)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What are cgroups (control groups) in Linux?
opts: A Linux kernel feature limiting and accounting for resource usage (CPU, memory, I/O, network) of process groups; used by Docker to enforce container resource limits
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: cgroups limit resource usage: CPU (shares/quota), memory (limit + OOM killer), block I/O, network. Docker uses cgroups to enforce container limits set by --memory, --cpus flags.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: cgroups = Linux resource limits (CPU/mem/IO) for container groups; Docker uses for limits

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is YAML and why is it used in DevOps?
opts: Yet Another Markup Language - web only
YAML Ain't Markup Language - a human-readable data serialisation format using indentation (spaces only) widely used for K8s, Docker Compose, Ansible, and CI/CD configs
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: YAML: human-friendly serialisation using spaces (no tabs). Used in: K8s manifests, Docker Compose, Ansible playbooks, GitHub Actions, Helm Charts, Terraform. Key: indentation matters.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: YAML = human-readable config; spaces not tabs; K8s/Compose/Ansible/GitHub Actions

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is continuous monitoring in DevOps?
