opts: Automated ongoing collection and analysis of metrics, logs, and traces across the pipeline and production to detect issues and trigger alerts 24x7
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Continuous Monitoring: automated 24x7 observability. Prometheus (metrics), Grafana (dashboards), ELK/Loki (logs), Jaeger (traces), AlertManager (alerts). Enables rapid incident response and SRE practices.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Continuous Monitoring = automated 24x7 metrics+logs+traces; Prometheus+Grafana+ELK stack

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is GitLab CI/CD?
opts: CI/CD built natively into GitLab, configured in .gitlab-ci.yml using GitLab Runners; requires no separate server unlike Jenkins
GitLab is open source; Jenkins is commercial
Jenkins is newer than GitLab CI
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: GitLab CI/CD: native to GitLab, configured in .gitlab-ci.yml, uses GitLab Runners. Zero extra setup on gitlab.com. Jenkins: standalone server, 1800+ plugins, supports any SCM, more customisable.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: GitLab CI = native (.gitlab-ci.yml + Runners); Jenkins = standalone server

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is SSH key-based authentication and why is it preferred in DevOps?
opts: Using cryptographic key pairs (public/private) instead of passwords for SSH; more secure and enables passwordless automation for Ansible, CI/CD, and Git
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: SSH keys: private key stays on client, public key on server (authorized_keys). More secure (no brute force), enables passwordless automation. Used by Ansible (SSH auth), CI/CD agents, git push.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: SSH keys = cryptographic key pair; more secure than passwords; enables automation

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a message queue in DevOps architecture?
opts: An asynchronous communication mechanism where producers send messages to a queue and consumers process them independently, decoupling services and handling traffic spikes
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Message queues (RabbitMQ, Kafka, AWS SQS): decouple producers and consumers. Benefits: buffer traffic spikes, retry failed processing, async workflows, prevent cascading failures in microservices.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Message queue = async decoupling; buffer spikes; RabbitMQ/Kafka/SQS; producer>queue>consumer

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the CAP Theorem?
opts: A distributed systems theorem: you can guarantee at most two of Consistency, Availability, and Partition Tolerance simultaneously
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: CAP Theorem (Brewer 2000): Consistency (all nodes see same data), Availability (every request gets response), Partition Tolerance (works despite network partitions). Real choice is CP or AP.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: CAP = Consistency + Availability + Partition tolerance; only 2 of 3; choose CP or AP

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is an API?
opts: Application Programming Interface - a set of rules and protocols allowing different software applications to communicate and share data
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: An API (Application Programming Interface) defines how software components interact. REST APIs use HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) and return JSON/XML. APIs enable microservices communication and cloud integration.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: API = interface for software communication; REST APIs use HTTP + JSON

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is DevOps monitoring and why does it matter?
opts: Continuously collecting, analysing, and alerting on system metrics, logs, and traces to detect failures early, reduce MTTR, and enable data-driven improvements
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: DevOps monitoring: metrics (Prometheus), logs (ELK/Loki), traces (Jaeger). Goals: detect failures early, understand system behaviour, enable data-driven improvements, support SLOs and reduce MTTR.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: DevOps monitoring = metrics+logs+traces; detect failures + reduce MTTR + support SLOs

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a reverse proxy and how is it used in DevOps?
opts: A server sitting in front of backend servers forwarding client requests, providing load balancing, SSL termination, caching, and hiding backend IPs
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Reverse proxy (NGINX, HAProxy): clients connect to proxy, proxy forwards to backend. Benefits: load balancing, SSL termination (decrypt HTTPS at proxy), caching, hide backend IPs, rate limiting.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Reverse proxy = in front of backends; load balance + SSL termination + cache + security

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is HashiCorp Vault?
opts: A secrets management tool securely storing and dynamically generating sensitive data (API keys, passwords, certificates) with fine-grained access control and audit logging
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: HashiCorp Vault: centralised secrets management. Features: dynamic secrets (short-lived credentials on-demand), secret rotation, audit logging, fine-grained policies, multiple auth methods (K8s, AWS IAM, LDAP).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Vault = secrets management; dynamic secrets + rotation + audit + K8s/AWS auth

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Trivy in DevOps?
opts: An open-source vulnerability scanner for container images, filesystems, and K8s detecting CVEs in packages, IaC misconfigurations, and secrets; trivy image myapp:latest
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Trivy (Aqua Security): comprehensive scanner for container image CVEs, IaC misconfiguration (K8s/Terraform/CF), secrets, SBOM generation. Easy CI/CD integration: trivy image myapp:latest.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Trivy = open-source vulnerability scanner for images/IaC/secrets; easy CI/CD integration

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the purpose of Kubernetes resource limits and requests?
opts: Requests = minimum resources guaranteed (used for scheduling); Limits = maximum resources a pod can use before being throttled (CPU) or killed (memory)
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: resources.requests: scheduler places pod on node with sufficient resources. resources.limits: CPU throttled if exceeded; memory OOMKilled if exceeded. Best practice: set both; requests <= limits.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: K8s requests = guaranteed minimum (scheduling); limits = maximum (killed if exceeded); set both

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is GitOps and what tools implement it?
opts: Using Git for source code only
A practice using Git as single truth for declarative infrastructure; ArgoCD and Flux are the main tools continuously reconciling cluster state to match Git
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: GitOps: all infrastructure and application config in Git. ArgoCD or Flux watches Git and automatically syncs cluster state to match. Benefits: audit trail, rollback via git revert, drift detection.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: GitOps = Git as truth; ArgoCD/Flux sync cluster to Git; audit trail + rollback + drift detection

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Kubernetes pod affinity and anti-affinity?
opts: Rules controlling pod scheduling based on labels of other pods: affinity attracts pods together; anti-affinity spreads them apart (e.g., HA across nodes)
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Pod affinity: schedule near pods with specific labels. Anti-affinity: schedule away (e.g., spread 3 replicas across 3 nodes for HA). Uses requiredDuringScheduling or preferredDuringScheduling.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Pod affinity = schedule near matching pods; anti-affinity = spread apart; used for HA placement

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Kubernetes Operator?
opts: A method of packaging and deploying Kubernetes apps using custom controllers (CRDs) that extend K8s API with domain-specific operational knowledge (e.g., managing DB clusters)
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: K8s Operators encode operational knowledge as code. Use CRDs (Custom Resource Definitions) + Custom Controllers. Examples: PostgreSQL Operator (backup/failover/scaling), Prometheus Operator, Cert-Manager.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: K8s Operator = custom controller + CRD; encodes operational knowledge (DB backup/failover)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Kubernetes network policy?
opts: A K8s resource controlling ingress and egress traffic between pods using label selectors for microsegmentation; requires a CNI plugin (Calico, Cilium, Weave) that supports it
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: NetworkPolicy: pod-level firewall using selectors. Default: all traffic allowed. With NetworkPolicy: explicitly allow needed traffic, deny rest. Requires compatible CNI: Calico, Cilium, Weave Net.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: NetworkPolicy = pod traffic control (ingress/egress); requires compatible CNI plugin

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Docker layer caching optimisation?
opts: Optimising Dockerfile instruction order so infrequently-changing instructions (package installs) come before frequently-changing ones (COPY source code) to maximise cache hits and speed builds
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Layer caching: if instruction and context unchanged, Docker reuses cached layer. Optimise: FROM > apt install (rarely changes) > COPY requirements.txt > pip install > COPY source (changes often).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Optimise layer caching: stable instructions first (packages), changing last (source code)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a rolling restart in Kubernetes?
opts: Sequentially restarting pods in a Deployment one-by-one to apply configuration changes (like new env vars or secrets) without downtime
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Rolling restart: kubectl rollout restart deployment/myapp. Sequentially replaces pods while maintaining availability. Used when pods need to pick up ConfigMap/Secret changes without changing image.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Rolling restart = kubectl rollout restart; sequential pod replacement; picks up config changes

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the purpose of Docker health checks?
opts: A Dockerfile HEALTHCHECK instruction Docker uses to periodically verify container health, marking it 'unhealthy' and potentially restarting it if checks fail
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: HEALTHCHECK in Dockerfile: HEALTHCHECK CMD curl -f http://localhost/health. Docker runs periodically. Failed checks mark container 'unhealthy'. K8s equivalent: livenessProbe. Compose: depends_on condition.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Docker HEALTHCHECK = periodic health verification; marks unhealthy + triggers restart policy

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Prometheus recording rule?
opts: A pre-computed PromQL expression stored as a new time-series metric, improving dashboard query performance for expensive/frequently-used expressions
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Recording rules: pre-compute expensive PromQL queries as new metrics. Instead of computing rate(http_requests_total[5m]) on every dashboard refresh, store as job:http_requests:rate5m. Significant perf improvement.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Recording rules = pre-compute expensive PromQL as new metrics; improves dashboard performance

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is 'git rebase' and how does it differ from merge?
opts: git rebase replays commits from one branch on top of another creating linear history (no merge commits); merge creates a merge commit preserving branch structure
git rebase for remote repos; merge for local
git rebase deletes history; merge preserves it
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: git rebase: moves/replays your branch's commits on top of another branch, creating linear history (no merge commits). Merge: preserves branch structure with a merge commit. Never rebase shared/public branches.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Rebase = linear history by replaying commits; merge = merge commit; never rebase public branches

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is AWS ECS (Elastic Container Service)?
opts: AWS's managed container orchestration service supporting both EC2-backed and Fargate (serverless) launch types for running Docker containers
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: ECS (Elastic Container Service): AWS's native container orchestration. Task Definitions (like Pods), Services (like Deployments), Clusters. Launch types: EC2 (manage nodes) or Fargate (serverless). Integrates with ALB, IAM, ECR.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: ECS = AWS native container orchestration; Task Definition + Service; EC2 or Fargate launch type

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is infrastructure drift and how do IaC tools handle it?
opts: When actual infrastructure state deviates from IaC code (due to manual changes); Terraform detects drift with 'plan', Ansible corrects it by re-applying idempotent playbooks
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Infrastructure drift: manual changes outside IaC (e.g., console changes). Terraform plan shows diff between state and real infra. Ansible re-apply corrects drift (idempotent). Immutable infra prevents drift.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Drift = actual infra != IaC code; terraform plan detects; Ansible re-apply corrects

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is continuous testing in DevOps?
opts: The practice of executing automated tests at every stage of the CI/CD pipeline (unit, integration, performance, security) to get immediate feedback on code quality
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Continuous testing: automated tests at every CI/CD stage. Unit tests (fastest), integration tests, contract tests, performance tests, security scans (SAST/DAST). Shift left - catch defects early and cheaply.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Continuous testing = automated tests at every CI/CD stage; shift left; catch defects early

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is shift-left security?
opts: Moving servers to the left data center
Integrating security practices and testing early in the development lifecycle (in IDE, during coding, in CI) rather than only at the end
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Shift-left security: move security earlier in SDLC. Code: pre-commit hooks, IDE plugins (Snyk, SonarLint). Build: SAST, dependency scanning. Test: DAST. Earlier = cheaper to fix vulnerabilities.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Shift-left security = integrate security early (code/build/test); cheaper + faster to fix

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the difference between Docker CMD and ENTRYPOINT?
opts: ENTRYPOINT sets the fixed executable (hard to override); CMD sets default arguments to ENTRYPOINT (easy to override at runtime); use together for flexible containers
CMD is for build; ENTRYPOINT is for runtime
CMD is older; ENTRYPOINT is newer
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: ENTRYPOINT: fixed executable, override only with --entrypoint flag. CMD: default args to ENTRYPOINT, easily overridden by docker run arguments. Together: ENTRYPOINT ['/app/start.sh'] CMD ['--debug'] pattern.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: ENTRYPOINT = fixed executable; CMD = default args (overridable); use together for flexibility

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Kubernetes StorageClass?
opts: A K8s resource defining storage types (SSD, HDD, NFS) with provisioners and parameters, enabling dynamic PV provisioning when a PVC requests storage
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: StorageClass: provisioner (aws-ebs, nfs-client), type (gp2, io1), reclaim policy (Delete/Retain). PVCs reference StorageClass for dynamic provisioning. Default StorageClass used when PVC doesn't specify one.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: StorageClass = defines storage type + provisioner; enables dynamic PV provisioning for PVCs

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the purpose of Kubernetes Taints and Tolerations?
opts: Taints on nodes repel all pods; Tolerations on pods allow scheduling on tainted nodes - used to dedicate nodes for specific workloads (GPU, high-memory)
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Taint node: kubectl taint nodes node1 key=value:NoSchedule. Only pods with matching Toleration can schedule there. Use cases: dedicate GPU nodes, mark nodes unschedulable, prevent general pods on masters.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Taints = node repels pods; Tolerations = pod allows tainted node; dedicate nodes to workloads

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the AWS Well-Architected Framework?
opts: A physical data center design guide
A set of best practices across five pillars (Operational Excellence, Security, Reliability, Performance Efficiency, Cost Optimization) for building cloud applications on AWS
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: AWS Well-Architected Framework: 5 pillars. Operational Excellence (automate operations), Security (protect data/systems), Reliability (recover from failures), Performance Efficiency (use resources efficiently), Cost Optimization (eliminate waste).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: AWS Well-Architected: Operational Excellence + Security + Reliability + Performance + Cost

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Kubernetes pod disruption budget (PDB)?
opts: A K8s resource guaranteeing a minimum number of pods remain available during voluntary disruptions (node drains, rolling updates) protecting application availability
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: PDB: minAvailable or maxUnavailable settings. During voluntary disruptions (kubectl drain), K8s won't remove pods if it would violate PDB. Prevents accidentally taking down too many pods at once.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: PDB = guarantee minimum pods available during voluntary disruptions (node drain/updates)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Ansible's idempotent approach compared to shell scripts?
opts: Ansible modules check current state before acting (install only if not installed), making operations safe to repeat; shell scripts always execute without checking current state
Ansible is slower than shell scripts
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Ansible idempotency: apt module checks if package installed before running apt-get install. Shell script: always runs apt-get install even if already installed. Ansible = safe to re-run; scripts = side effects.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Ansible = idempotent (check before act); shell scripts = always execute; Ansible safer to re-run

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the purpose of Docker's EXPOSE instruction?
opts: Blocks access to the specified port
Documents which ports the container application listens on; does not actually publish the port - use -p or ports: in Compose to publish
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: EXPOSE documents intended ports. Does NOT publish them. To publish: docker run -p 8080:80 (CLI) or ports: ['8080:80'] in Compose. EXPOSE is documentation + used by --publish-all flag.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: EXPOSE = documents ports (not publish); use -p or ports: to actually publish

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is kubectl exec?
opts: A command that opens an interactive shell or runs a command inside a running container: kubectl exec -it pod-name -- /bin/bash
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: kubectl exec -it pod-name -- /bin/bash: opens interactive shell inside running container. -it: interactive TTY. Replace /bin/bash with /bin/sh for minimal images. Useful for debugging running containers.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: kubectl exec -it = open shell inside running container; essential debugging tool

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Kubernetes readiness probe?
opts: A periodic check determining if a container is ready to serve traffic; if it fails, the pod is removed from Service endpoints but NOT restarted (unlike liveness probe)
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Readiness probe: should pod receive traffic? Types: HTTP GET, TCP, Exec. Failed = pod removed from Service endpoint load balancing. Used for: slow-starting apps, maintenance mode, dependency not ready.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Readiness probe = ready to receive traffic?; failed probe removes pod from LB (not restart)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the difference between Docker Compose and Docker Swarm?
opts: Docker Compose orchestrates multi-container apps on a single host (development); Docker Swarm orchestrates containers across multiple hosts in a production cluster
Compose is newer; Swarm is older
Compose is for production; Swarm is for development
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Docker Compose: single-host multi-container orchestration for development and testing. Docker Swarm: multi-host production cluster with manager nodes, service scaling, and rolling updates.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Compose = single-host (dev); Swarm = multi-host cluster (production); different use cases

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Infrastructure as Code testing?
opts: The practice of writing automated tests for IaC code (Terraform/Ansible) to validate correctness before applying to production; tools: Terratest, Kitchen-Terraform, Molecule
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: IaC testing: validate infrastructure code before applying. Terraform: Terratest (Go tests that actually provision and verify), Kitchen-Terraform. Ansible: Molecule (test roles in containers/VMs). Part of CI/CD pipeline.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: IaC testing = automated tests for infra code; Terratest (Terraform), Molecule (Ansible)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is AWS VPC peering?
opts: A networking connection between two VPCs enabling private IP routing between them as if they were in the same network; works within region and cross-region
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: VPC peering: connect two VPCs for private traffic routing. No internet gateway, VPN, or physical device needed. Works within same region or cross-region and cross-account. No transitive peering.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: VPC peering = private connection between two VPCs; no internet; cross-region/account capable

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Ansible's 'when' conditional?
opts: A task attribute that conditionally executes a task only when the specified expression evaluates to true; enables environment-specific task execution
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: when: conditional execution. Example: when: ansible_os_family == 'Debian' (run apt task only on Debian). when: inventory_hostname in groups['webservers'] (run only on webservers). Supports complex expressions.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Ansible 'when' = conditional task execution based on expressions; environment-specific tasks

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Docker's overlay network?
opts: A Docker network driver enabling container communication across multiple Docker hosts in a Swarm; creates a distributed network spanning the cluster
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Docker overlay network: used in Swarm mode to connect containers across multiple hosts. Docker handles the encryption and routing transparently. Services on same overlay network communicate by name.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Docker overlay network = multi-host container communication in Swarm; transparent routing

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Terraform's 'count' meta-argument?
opts: A Terraform meta-argument creating multiple instances of a resource based on a numeric value; enables creating N identical resources without repeating configuration
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: count = N creates N instances of a resource. Access individual: aws_instance.web[0]. For non-identical resources, use for_each (map/set) instead. count.index provides the current index (0-based).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Terraform count = create N identical resources; access with resource[index]; prefer for_each for maps

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the ITIL Service Lifecycle?
opts: A framework of five stages: Service Strategy, Service Design, Service Transition, Service Operation, and Continual Service Improvement
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: ITIL Service Lifecycle: Strategy (why?), Design (what?), Transition (how to deliver?), Operation (deliver and support), CSI (how to improve?). Together they provide end-to-end IT service management.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: ITIL Lifecycle: Strategy > Design > Transition > Operation > CSI

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a service request in ITIL?
opts: A formal user request for something new or standard - information, advice, access, or a standard change (not an incident)
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: A Service Request is a formal user request that is predefined, low risk, and pre-approved. Examples: password reset, new user account, software installation, information request. Handled by Service Desk.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Service Request = predefined, pre-approved user request; password reset, access grant etc.

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is proactive Problem Management in ITIL?
opts: Reacting to incidents after they occur
Identifying and preventing problems and known errors before incidents occur by analysing trends, infrastructure, and service data
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Proactive Problem Management analyses trends (frequent incidents on same CI), infrastructure weaknesses, and CSI data to identify and eliminate potential problems before they cause incidents.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Proactive Problem Management = prevent incidents before they occur; trend analysis

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the difference between Warranty and Utility in ITIL?
opts: Utility = what a service does (fit for purpose); Warranty = how well it does it (fit for use: available, capacity, continuity, security)
Utility is financial; Warranty is technical
Utility is for customers; Warranty is for IT
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Utility (fit for purpose): service supports required performance or removes constraints. Warranty (fit for use): service is available when needed, with sufficient capacity, continuous, and secure. Both needed for value.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Utility = fit for purpose (what it does); Warranty = fit for use (available, capacity, secure)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What does 'shift-left' mean in ITIL CSI?
opts: Moving improvement activities and testing earlier in the service lifecycle to prevent issues rather than fix them after they occur
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: In ITIL context, shift-left means resolving issues at lower cost, lower skill levels, and earlier in the process. Service Desk resolves more (fewer escalations), CSI addresses root causes earlier.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Shift-left in ITIL = resolve at lower level, earlier in process; reduce escalations and costs

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Configuration Item (CI) in ITIL?
opts: Any component that needs to be managed in order to deliver an IT service; can be hardware, software, documentation, people, or buildings
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: A CI (Configuration Item) is any component managed in the CMDB. Types: hardware (servers, routers), software (OS, applications), documentation (SLAs), people (roles), services. Each has attributes and relationships.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: CI = any component managed in CMDB; hardware, software, docs, people, services

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the Deming Cycle (PDCA)?
opts: Plan-Do-Check-Act: an iterative four-step management method for continuous improvement of processes and products
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: PDCA: Plan (identify opportunity, plan change), Do (implement change on small scale), Check (measure results against expected), Act (standardise if successful or start over). Core of ITIL CSI.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: PDCA = Plan-Do-Check-Act; iterative improvement cycle; core of ITIL CSI

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Major Incident in ITIL?
opts: A high-priority incident with significant business impact requiring a separate major incident procedure with war room, dedicated team, and executive communication
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Major Incident: significant impact on business (critical systems down, many users affected). Requires: dedicated major incident team, frequent updates, workaround focus, post-incident review, and executive communication.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Major Incident = high-impact incident; dedicated team, frequent updates, post-incident review

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a workaround in ITIL?
opts: A permanent fix for a problem
A temporary means of overcoming an incident or problem to restore service, even if the underlying root cause remains unresolved
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: A workaround restores service or reduces impact without resolving the underlying problem. Example: restart a hung service (workaround) while investigating why it hangs (problem management).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Workaround = temporary fix to restore service; root cause still unresolved

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Continual Service Improvement (CSI) in ITIL?
opts: An ITIL lifecycle phase that uses measurement and analysis to identify and implement improvements to IT services and processes on an ongoing basis
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: CSI is ITIL's ongoing improvement phase. Uses: CSI Register, 7-step improvement process, PDCA, metrics (CSFs and KPIs), and benchmarking to continually improve service quality and process efficiency.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: CSI = ongoing improvement of services/processes; uses PDCA, KPIs, CSI Register

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Tier III data center's key characteristic?
opts: Concurrent maintainability: all components are redundant and maintainable without shutting down; 99.982% uptime
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Tier III: all components are redundant (N+1 or better), and any component can be maintained without taking the data center offline. 99.982% uptime = ~1.6 hours downtime/year.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Tier III = concurrent maintainability; all components redundant + serviceable without shutdown

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Tier IV data center's key characteristic?
opts: Fault tolerant: can sustain any single failure including a full power path failure without impacting operations; 99.995% uptime (~26 min downtime/year)
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Tier IV: fault tolerant, sustains any single failure including simultaneous loss of both power paths. 2N+1 power and cooling. 99.995% uptime = ~26 minutes downtime/year. Highest data center tier.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Tier IV = fault tolerant; sustains single failure on any system; 99.995% uptime

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a CRAC unit in a data center?
opts: Computer Room Air Conditioner - a precision cooling unit designed specifically for data centers to maintain temperature and humidity within tight tolerances
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: CRAC (Computer Room Air Conditioner) units are precision air conditioning systems designed for data centers, maintaining tight temperature (18-27°C) and humidity (40-60% RH) to protect equipment.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: CRAC = Computer Room Air Conditioner; precision cooling for data centers

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a PDU in a data center?
opts: Power Distribution Unit - a device distributing electrical power from a UPS or generator to servers and equipment in server racks
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: PDU (Power Distribution Unit): distributes power from a single source (UPS/generator) to multiple devices in server racks. Types: basic (power strip), metered (measure consumption), switched (remote control each outlet).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: PDU = Power Distribution Unit; distributes power from UPS to rack equipment

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is N+1 redundancy in data centers?
opts: A redundancy model where one additional redundant component exists beyond the minimum required (N = minimum needed, +1 = spare); failure of any one component doesn't affect service
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: N+1 redundancy: if you need N components to run, you have N+1 (one spare). Example: need 3 UPS units (N=3), have 4 (N+1=4). One failure doesn't affect service. Tier III uses N+1 or better.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: N+1 redundancy = one spare beyond minimum needed; one failure doesn't affect service

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a hot spare in a data center?
opts: A spare server kept at high temperature
A fully powered and operational backup component that can immediately take over if the primary component fails, with no interruption
A spare component kept in storage
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Hot spare: fully powered and active, ready to take over instantly if the primary fails. Contrast: Cold spare (stored, not powered), Warm spare (powered but not fully active, some delay). Used for critical components.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Hot spare = fully active backup; instant takeover; vs cold spare (stored) and warm spare

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is disaster recovery (DR) in IT?
opts: A set of policies, tools, and procedures to enable the recovery of IT infrastructure and systems following a disaster
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: DR plans for recovery of IT systems after disasters. Key metrics: RTO (Recovery Time Objective - how long to recover), RPO (Recovery Point Objective - how much data loss acceptable). Includes backup, failover, and testing.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: DR = policies + tools for IT recovery after disaster; defined by RTO and RPO

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is RPO (Recovery Point Objective)?
opts: The maximum acceptable amount of data loss measured in time; how far back in time can you afford to recover data
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: RPO: maximum tolerable data loss. RPO of 1 hour = tolerate losing up to 1 hour of data. Drives backup frequency. Lower RPO = more frequent backups = higher cost. Paired with RTO.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: RPO = maximum acceptable data loss in time; drives backup frequency; lower = more frequent backup

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is RTO (Recovery Time Objective)?
opts: The maximum acceptable duration of time to restore service after a disaster; how long can the business tolerate the service being down
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: RTO: maximum tolerable downtime. RTO of 4 hours = must restore service within 4 hours of a disaster. Drives DR architecture (hot standby vs cold standby). Lower RTO = higher cost.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: RTO = maximum acceptable downtime duration; drives DR architecture design

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a colocation data center?
opts: A data center for one company
A facility where businesses rent space (racks, cages, cabinets) and power to host their own hardware while the provider manages the building, power, cooling, and connectivity
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Colocation (colo): businesses bring their own servers to the provider's facility. Provider supplies: space, power (with redundancy), cooling, physical security, and internet connectivity. Customer manages hardware/software.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Colocation = rent space+power+cooling at provider's DC; you own and manage the hardware

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is VMware vSphere?
opts: VMware's enterprise virtualisation platform combining ESXi (hypervisor) and vCenter Server (management) to create and manage virtualised data centers
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: vSphere is VMware's enterprise virtualisation suite: ESXi (Type 1 hypervisor on each host), vCenter Server (centralised management of multiple ESXi hosts, VMs, and clusters), vSphere Client (web UI).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: vSphere = VMware's enterprise virtualisation platform: ESXi hypervisor + vCenter management

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is KVM (Kernel-based Virtual Machine)?
opts: A Type 1 hypervisor built into the Linux kernel (since 2.6.20) that turns Linux into a bare-metal hypervisor; open-source and used in cloud platforms
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: KVM is a Linux kernel module making Linux a Type 1 hypervisor. Combined with QEMU (device emulation), it creates and manages VMs. Used by: AWS (Nitro), Google Cloud, OpenStack. Requires Intel VT-x or AMD-V.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: KVM = Linux kernel hypervisor (Type 1); used by AWS/GCP/OpenStack; needs VT-x/AMD-V

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is live migration (vMotion) in virtualisation?
opts: Restarting a VM on another host
Moving a running VM from one physical host to another with zero downtime; the VM continues running during the entire migration
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Live migration (VMware vMotion, KVM live migration): moves running VMs between hosts without downtime. Copies memory pages to destination host, then transfers CPU state and disk access. Users experience no interruption.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Live migration = move running VM between hosts with zero downtime; vMotion in VMware

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a virtual switch (vSwitch) in virtualisation?
opts: A software-defined switch running inside a hypervisor that connects VMs to each other and to physical network interfaces
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Virtual switch (VMware vSwitch, Linux Bridge, Open vSwitch): software-defined switching for VMs. Connects VMs to each other within the same host and to physical NICs for external connectivity. Supports VLANs.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: vSwitch = software switch inside hypervisor; connects VMs to each other and physical network

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is NUMA (Non-Uniform Memory Access) in virtualisation?
opts: A memory architecture where CPUs access their local memory faster than memory on other nodes; important for VM placement to avoid performance penalties
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: NUMA: multi-socket servers have NUMA nodes (each socket + its local RAM). VMs spanning NUMA nodes have higher memory latency. Best practice: allocate VMs within a single NUMA node for best performance.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: NUMA = local memory access faster; align VMs to single NUMA node for best performance

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is over-provisioning in virtualisation?
opts: Allocating more virtual resources (CPU, RAM) to VMs than the physical host actually has, relying on the fact that not all VMs use 100% simultaneously
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Over-provisioning: allocate more vCPU/RAM than physical CPUs/RAM exist. Works because VMs rarely all hit 100% simultaneously. Increases VM density but risks performance issues under heavy load.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Over-provisioning = allocate more virtual resources than physical; works at low utilisation

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a VM escape in security?
opts: A security vulnerability where malicious code in a VM breaks out of its isolation and gains access to the hypervisor or other VMs on the same host
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: VM escape: attacker's code running in a guest VM exploits hypervisor vulnerability to break out of isolation. Extremely serious - can compromise all VMs on the host. Regular hypervisor patches are critical.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: VM escape = breaking out of VM isolation to access hypervisor/other VMs; serious vulnerability

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is thin provisioning vs thick provisioning?
opts: Thin provisioning: allocates disk space on-demand as data is written (efficient); Thick provisioning: pre-allocates all disk space upfront (predictable, better performance)
Thin is faster; thick is slower
Thick is newer; thin is older
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Thin provisioning: VM disk file grows as data is written (efficient, overcommit possible). Thick eager zeroed: pre-allocates and zeros all space (best performance, no growth delays). Thick lazy: pre-allocates, zeros on first write.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Thin = on-demand allocation; Thick = pre-allocated space; trade-off: efficiency vs performance

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a LUN (Logical Unit Number) in SAN?
opts: A logical partition of storage presented to a server over a SAN, appearing as a local disk; multiple LUNs can come from the same physical storage array
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: A LUN is a logical storage unit presented by a storage array to a server via SAN. The server sees it as a local disk. Multiple LUNs can be carved from RAID groups or pools on the same storage array.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: LUN = logical storage partition presented to server over SAN; appears as local disk

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is RAID and why is it used in storage?
opts: Redundant Array of Independent Disks - combines multiple physical disks for performance and/or redundancy; RAID 1 (mirror), RAID 5 (parity), RAID 6 (dual parity), RAID 10 (mirror+stripe)
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: RAID: combine multiple disks. RAID 0 (stripe, speed, no redundancy), RAID 1 (mirror, full redundancy), RAID 5 (parity, can lose 1 disk), RAID 6 (dual parity, can lose 2 disks), RAID 10 (stripe+mirror).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: RAID = multiple disks for redundancy/performance; RAID1=mirror, RAID5=parity, RAID10=stripe+mirror

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Fibre Channel (FC) in SAN?
opts: A high-speed dedicated network protocol for SAN storage, providing 8/16/32 Gbps block storage access with very low latency; typically uses FC switches and HBAs
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Fibre Channel: dedicated SAN protocol. Uses HBAs (Host Bus Adapters) on servers and FC switches. Speeds: 8Gbps (8GFC), 16Gbps (16GFC), 32Gbps (32GFC). Very low latency, high reliability. Used in enterprise data centers.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Fibre Channel = dedicated high-speed SAN protocol (8/16/32 Gbps); HBAs + FC switches

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is an HBA (Host Bus Adapter)?
opts: A specialised network card in a server that connects it to a Fibre Channel or iSCSI SAN; provides the interface between the server and the storage network
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: HBA (Host Bus Adapter): specialised card providing server connectivity to SAN. FC HBA: connects to Fibre Channel switches. iSCSI HBA (or software initiator): connects to iSCSI SAN over Ethernet. Exposes LUNs to the OS.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: HBA = specialised card connecting server to SAN (FC or iSCSI); exposes LUNs to OS

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is deduplication in storage?
opts: A storage optimisation technique that identifies and eliminates duplicate data blocks, storing only one copy of repeated data to reduce storage capacity requirements
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Deduplication (dedup): detects identical data blocks and stores only one copy with references. Reduces storage by 2:1 to 10:1 or more for VMs, backups, file servers. Inline (real-time) or post-process.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Deduplication = store one copy of duplicate blocks; reduces storage 2:1 to 10:1 for VMs/backup

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is storage tiering?
opts: Automatically moving data between different storage tiers (fast SSD, mid-tier SAS, slow SATA) based on access frequency to optimise cost and performance
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Storage tiering: hot data (frequently accessed) on fast SSDs, warm data on SAS/NL-SAS, cold data on cheap SATA or tape. Automated tiering moves data between tiers based on access patterns.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Storage tiering = move hot data to fast storage (SSD), cold data to cheap storage (SATA/tape)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is object storage vs block storage?
opts: Block storage: raw fixed-size blocks, no metadata, accessed by servers as local disks (SAN); Object storage: data stored as objects with metadata and unique ID, accessed via HTTP/API (S3)
Object is faster; block is older
Block is for cloud; object is for on-premise
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Block storage (SAN/EBS): raw blocks, OS manages filesystem, high performance, ideal for databases. Object storage (S3/Ceph): objects with metadata + unique ID, accessed via REST API, massive scale, cheap, eventually consistent.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Block = raw blocks for OS filesystem (DB/VM); Object = metadata+ID, HTTP API, massive scale (S3)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is NFS (Network File System)?
opts: A distributed file system protocol allowing clients to mount remote filesystems over a network, commonly used for NAS storage on Linux systems
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: NFS (Network File System): allows Linux/Unix clients to mount remote directories as if they were local. Server exports directory; clients mount it via NFS protocol. Common for NAS storage, shared home directories, Kubernetes PVs.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: NFS = mount remote filesystems over network; common for Linux NAS and K8s persistent volumes

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is SMB/CIFS in storage?
opts: Server Message Block / Common Internet File System - a network file sharing protocol used primarily by Windows for accessing shared folders and NAS storage
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: SMB (Server Message Block), also called CIFS, is Windows' native file sharing protocol. Used by: Windows network shares, Samba (Linux SMB server), NAS appliances sharing to Windows clients.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: SMB/CIFS = Windows network file sharing protocol; NAS shares to Windows clients via SMB

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is cloud bursting?
opts: A hybrid cloud pattern where applications run in a private cloud but automatically scale into a public cloud when demand exceeds private cloud capacity
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Cloud bursting: normally runs in private cloud. When demand exceeds capacity, workloads automatically overflow (burst) into public cloud (AWS/Azure/GCP). Cost-effective: private cloud for base load, public for peaks.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Cloud bursting = private cloud + overflow to public cloud for peak demand; hybrid pattern

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a cloud region?
opts: A geographic area containing multiple Availability Zones (data centers); regions are isolated from each other for fault isolation and compliance
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Cloud region: geographic area with multiple AZs (Availability Zones). AWS regions: us-east-1, eu-west-1, ap-south-1. Regions are fully isolated (disasters, regulations). Choose region based on latency and compliance.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Cloud region = geographic area with multiple AZs; choose for latency and compliance

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a cloud Availability Zone (AZ)?
opts: One or more discrete data centers within a region with independent power, cooling, and networking; designing across multiple AZs enables high availability
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Availability Zone: isolated data center(s) within a region. Multiple AZs in a region are connected by low-latency links. Deploy across multiple AZs for HA: if one AZ fails, other AZs continue serving traffic.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Availability Zone = isolated DC within region; deploy across multiple AZs for HA

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is cloud cost optimisation?
opts: Strategies to reduce cloud spend without sacrificing performance: right-sizing instances, using Reserved/Spot instances, deleting unused resources, using auto-scaling, and monitoring with cost explorer
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Cloud cost optimisation: right-size (not over-provisioned), use Reserved Instances (commit for discount), Spot Instances (cheapest), delete idle resources, use auto-scaling, set budgets and alerts, use cost explorer.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Cloud cost optimisation: right-size + Reserved/Spot + delete idle + auto-scale + monitor spend

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS) pricing model?
opts: Pay-as-you-go (pay per second/hour for compute, per GB for storage, per GB transferred for networking); no upfront hardware purchase
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: IaaS pricing: pay-as-you-go for compute (per second), storage (per GB), network (per GB egress). Eliminates CapEx (hardware purchases). Replace with OpEx (operating expenses). Can reserve for discounts.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: IaaS pricing = pay-as-you-go (per second/GB); no upfront CapEx; convert to OpEx

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a cloud security shared responsibility model?
opts: Cloud provider is responsible for everything
A model defining security responsibilities: provider secures the infrastructure (physical hardware, hypervisor, networking); customer secures their data, OS, applications, and access management
Security is not needed in cloud
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Shared responsibility: Provider = physical security, hardware, hypervisor, network infrastructure. Customer = OS patching, application security, data encryption, IAM configuration, network security groups.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Shared responsibility: provider secures infrastructure; customer secures data/OS/apps/IAM

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is multi-cloud strategy?
opts: Using multiple accounts on one cloud
Using services from multiple cloud providers (AWS + Azure + GCP) to avoid vendor lock-in, leverage best-of-breed services, and improve resilience
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Multi-cloud: use AWS for compute, Azure for Office 365 integration, GCP for ML/AI. Benefits: avoid vendor lock-in, leverage each provider's strengths, regulatory compliance, resilience. Challenges: complexity, skills.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Multi-cloud = use multiple cloud providers; avoid lock-in; leverage best-of-breed services

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is edge computing?
opts: Computing at the edge of a table
Processing data closer to where it is generated (at the edge of the network, near IoT devices) rather than sending all data to a central cloud, reducing latency
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Edge computing: process data near the source (factory sensors, retail stores, autonomous vehicles) instead of sending all data to cloud. Reduces latency, bandwidth, and dependency on cloud connectivity.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Edge computing = process data near the source; reduces latency + bandwidth; IoT/autonomous

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a cloud-native application?
opts: An application running in a cloud
An application designed specifically for cloud environments: containerised, microservices-based, using cloud services (managed DBs, queues, storage), elastic scaling, and CI/CD deployment
A traditional application moved to cloud
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Cloud-native: designed for cloud from ground up. Characteristics: containerised (Docker/K8s), microservices architecture, uses managed cloud services, stateless (12-factor app), elastic auto-scaling, CI/CD deployment.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Cloud-native = built for cloud; containerised + microservices + managed services + elastic + CI/CD

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is AWS Route 53?
opts: AWS's highly available and scalable Domain Name System (DNS) web service for routing traffic to AWS resources and external endpoints
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Route 53: AWS DNS service. Features: domain registration, DNS routing (simple, weighted, latency-based, failover, geolocation), health checks, traffic policies. Integrates with ELB, CloudFront, S3 static sites.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Route 53 = AWS DNS service; domain registration + traffic routing + health checks

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is AWS RDS (Relational Database Service)?
opts: A managed relational database service supporting MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL Server, and MariaDB with automated backups, patching, and Multi-AZ for HA
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: RDS: managed relational DB. Handles: provisioning, patching, backup, recovery, failover. Multi-AZ: synchronous standby replica in another AZ. Read replicas: async copies for read scaling. Supported: MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL Server.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: RDS = AWS managed relational DB; auto backup/patching/failover; Multi-AZ for HA

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is AWS DynamoDB?
opts: AWS's fully managed, serverless NoSQL key-value and document database providing single-digit millisecond performance at any scale
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: DynamoDB: serverless NoSQL. Fully managed (no servers to provision). Key-value + document. Unlimited scale (auto-scaling). Single-digit ms latency. Pay-per-request or provisioned capacity. Global Tables for multi-region.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: DynamoDB = AWS serverless NoSQL; key-value+document; unlimited scale; single-digit ms latency

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is AWS CloudFront?
opts: AWS's Content Delivery Network (CDN) that caches content at 400+ edge locations globally, reducing latency and origin load
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: CloudFront is AWS's CDN. Caches content (images, CSS, JS, video) at 400+ global Points of Presence (PoPs). Reduces latency by serving from nearest edge. Integrates with S3, ELB, API Gateway. Supports HTTPS.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: CloudFront = AWS CDN; 400+ edge locations; caches content near users; reduces latency

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is AWS Elastic Load Balancer (ELB)?
opts: AWS's managed load balancing service distributing traffic across multiple targets; types: ALB (HTTP/HTTPS L7), NLB (TCP/UDP L4), GLB (gateway L3)
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: ELB types: Application LB (L7, HTTP/HTTPS, path/host routing, integrates with WAF), Network LB (L4, TCP/UDP, ultra-high performance), Gateway LB (L3, network appliance integration). All integrate with Auto Scaling.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: ELB = AWS managed load balancer; ALB (L7 HTTP), NLB (L4 TCP), GLB (L3 gateway)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is AWS IAM Role?
opts: An AWS identity with permissions that can be assumed by AWS services, EC2 instances, Lambda functions, or other accounts - eliminates need for access keys on EC2
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: IAM Role: identity with policies that can be assumed by: EC2 (instance profile), Lambda, ECS task, cross-account. Best practice: use roles (not access keys) for EC2/Lambda. Roles have temporary credentials.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: IAM Role = assumable identity with permissions; EC2/Lambda use roles (not access keys)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is AWS ElastiCache?
opts: AWS's managed in-memory caching service supporting Redis and Memcached for reducing database load and improving application performance
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: ElastiCache: managed Redis or Memcached. Use cases: session storage, database query caching, real-time leaderboards, pub/sub messaging. Redis supports: persistence, replication, cluster mode, Lua scripts.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: ElastiCache = AWS managed Redis/Memcached; reduce DB load; session caching

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Docker registry vs Docker Hub?
opts: Docker registry is the technology (open standard for storing images); Docker Hub is Docker Inc's public hosted registry service; you can run private registries with the registry image
Registry is private; Hub is public
Registry is older; Hub is newer
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Docker Registry: software for storing and distributing Docker images (open-source, run yourself). Docker Hub: Docker's hosted public registry service (free public repos, paid private). Other registries: ECR, ACR, Harbor.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Docker Registry = image storage tech; Docker Hub = Docker's hosted registry service

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is the docker-compose.yml 'depends_on' directive?
opts: Specifies service startup order (service A waits for service B to start) but does NOT wait for B to be ready; use health checks + depends_on condition for true readiness
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: depends_on: orders container startup but only waits for container to START, not for service inside to be READY. For true readiness: combine with healthcheck and depends_on condition: service_healthy.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: depends_on = startup order only; use healthcheck + condition:service_healthy for readiness

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Docker BuildKit?
opts: A Docker build engine (enabled by default in Docker 23+) providing: parallel stage builds, better caching, secret mounting (no secrets in layers), and faster builds
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: BuildKit: next-generation Docker build engine. Features: parallel stage builds (multi-stage faster), improved caching, RUN --mount=type=secret (mount secrets without baking into layers), provenance attestation.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: BuildKit = modern Docker build engine; parallel builds + better cache + secret mounts

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a container runtime?
opts: The software responsible for running containers - pulling images, creating namespaces, managing cgroups, and starting processes; examples: containerd, CRI-O, Docker Engine
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Container runtime: low-level software running containers. Docker Engine uses containerd (+ runc). Kubernetes uses CRI (Container Runtime Interface): containerd, CRI-O. runc is the OCI-standard low-level runtime.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Container runtime = runs containers (containerd, CRI-O, Docker Engine); low-level execution

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is OCI (Open Container Initiative)?
opts: An open standards body (Linux Foundation) defining image format, distribution, and runtime specifications ensuring containers work across different runtimes and registries
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: OCI (Open Container Initiative): Linux Foundation project defining open standards for containers. OCI Image Spec (image format), OCI Runtime Spec (container execution), OCI Distribution Spec (registry API). Ensures interoperability.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: OCI = open container standards; image + runtime + distribution specs; ensures interoperability

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Docker's --restart policy?
opts: A container configuration defining restart behaviour: no (default), always (restart on any stop), on-failure (restart on non-zero exit), unless-stopped (restart unless manually stopped)
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Restart policies: no (default, never restart), always (always restart), on-failure[:max-retries] (restart on failure only), unless-stopped (restart always except when manually stopped). Set in docker run or compose.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Docker --restart = define container restart behaviour; always/on-failure/unless-stopped

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a distroless Docker image?
opts: A minimal container image containing only the application and its runtime dependencies, without a package manager, shell, or other OS tools - improving security by reducing attack surface
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Distroless images (Google's gcr.io/distroless): no package manager, no shell, no OS utilities. Only application + runtime dependencies. Security: no shell = harder to exploit. Smaller: fewer vulnerabilities to patch.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Distroless = no shell/package manager; only app + runtime; smaller + more secure

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Docker Swarm vs Kubernetes trade-off?
opts: Docker Swarm: simpler setup, native Docker integration, less features; Kubernetes: complex setup, more powerful, industry standard, self-healing, advanced scheduling, huge ecosystem
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Swarm: simpler (docker service create), built into Docker, good for small clusters, limited features. K8s: complex setup, powerful (RBAC, network policies, CRDs, operators), industry standard, huge ecosystem. K8s wins for production.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Swarm = simpler setup, limited features; K8s = complex, powerful, industry standard for production

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is docker system prune?
opts: A command cleaning up unused Docker resources: stopped containers, dangling images, unused networks, and optionally unused volumes to reclaim disk space
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: docker system prune: removes stopped containers, dangling images (no tag), unused networks. Add -a to remove ALL unused images (not just dangling). Add --volumes to include volumes. Reclaims significant disk space.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: docker system prune = clean up unused resources (stopped containers, dangling images, networks)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is container image scanning?
opts: Analysing Docker images for known security vulnerabilities (CVEs) in OS packages and language libraries before deploying to production; tools: Trivy, Snyk, Clair, AWS Inspector
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Image scanning: automated CVE detection in Docker images. Tools: Trivy (trivy image myapp:latest), Snyk, Clair, AWS ECR scanning. Integrate into CI/CD to block deployment of vulnerable images.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Image scanning = CVE detection in Docker images; Trivy/Snyk; integrate in CI/CD

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a merge conflict in Git?
opts: A situation where Git cannot automatically merge two branches because both made changes to the same part of a file; requires manual resolution
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Merge conflict: two branches modified the same lines in a file. Git marks conflicts with <<<<<<, =======, >>>>>>>. Developer must manually edit the file, choose correct content, stage, and commit to complete merge.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Merge conflict = same lines changed in both branches; manual resolution with <<<< ==== >>>>

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is 'git reset' and what are its modes?
opts: A way to delete the repository
A command moving HEAD (and optionally index/working tree) to a previous commit; modes: --soft (keep staged), --mixed (default, unstage), --hard (discard all changes)
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: git reset: --soft (move HEAD only, keep staged changes), --mixed (default: unstage changes, keep in working dir), --hard (discard ALL changes - dangerous, can lose work). Use git revert for public history.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: git reset: --soft (keep staged) / --mixed (unstage) / --hard (discard all); don't reset public history

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is 'git revert'?
opts: Creates a new commit that undoes the changes of a specific commit, preserving history; safe for public/shared branches unlike git reset
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: git revert <commit>: creates a new commit that reverses the specified commit's changes. Safe for shared branches (doesn't rewrite history). Contrast: git reset rewrites history (only safe for local changes).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: git revert = new commit undoing previous commit; safe for public branches; preserves history

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is trunk-based development?
opts: A branching strategy where all developers commit directly to a single main branch (trunk) daily, using feature flags for incomplete features; enables CI and rapid releases
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Trunk-based development: developers commit to main branch daily (or use very short-lived branches < 1 day). Feature flags hide incomplete work. No long-lived feature branches. Enables true CI. Requires strong automated testing.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Trunk-based = daily commits to main; feature flags hide incomplete features; enables true CI

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a GitHub fork?
opts: A personal copy of another user's repository that lives in your GitHub account; enables contributing to projects you don't have write access to via Pull Requests
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Fork: creates your own copy of a repository under your GitHub account. You can freely experiment, then contribute back via Pull Requests to the original. Standard open-source contribution workflow.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: GitHub Fork = personal copy of another's repo; contribute back via Pull Requests; open-source workflow

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is semantic versioning (SemVer)?
opts: A versioning scheme using MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH: increment MAJOR for breaking changes, MINOR for new features (backwards compatible), PATCH for bug fixes
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: SemVer: MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH. MAJOR: breaking API changes (2.0.0). MINOR: backwards-compatible new features (1.1.0). PATCH: backwards-compatible bug fixes (1.0.1). Pre-release: 1.0.0-alpha.1.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: SemVer = MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH; breaking.feature.bugfix; standard for versioning

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Git remote?
opts: A named reference to a remote repository URL; 'origin' is the default name for the repository you cloned from; you can have multiple remotes (origin, upstream)
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Git remote: named pointer to a remote repository URL. git remote -v (list), git remote add upstream URL (add), git remote remove name (remove). 'origin' = where you cloned from. 'upstream' = original repo in fork workflow.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Git remote = named URL pointer to remote repo; origin=cloned from; upstream=original in fork

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is 'git log' used for?
opts: Viewing the commit history of a repository showing commit hashes, author, date, and message; --oneline for compact view, --graph for branch visualisation
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: git log: shows commit history. Useful options: --oneline (one line per commit), --graph (ASCII branch graph), --all (show all branches), -n 10 (last 10 commits), --author='name', --since='2024-01-01'.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: git log = view commit history; --oneline compact; --graph visualise branches

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Jenkins Declarative Pipeline?
opts: A structured, opinionated pipeline syntax using pipeline{} block with stages{} and steps{} that is easier to read and write than scripted pipelines
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Declarative pipeline: pipeline{} > agent{} > stages{} > stage('name'){} > steps{} > sh/echo/etc. Provides built-in validation, better error messages, and post{} blocks for cleanup. Recommended for most use cases.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Declarative pipeline = pipeline{agent; stages{stage{steps}}}; structured, validated, recommended

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Jenkins Shared Library?
opts: A reusable code library stored in a Git repository and loaded by Jenkins pipelines to share common functionality (build steps, deployment logic) across multiple Jenkinsfiles
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Shared Library: Groovy code stored in a separate Git repo, loaded in Jenkinsfile via @Library('my-lib'). Contains vars/ (global functions) and src/ (Groovy classes). Enables DRY pipelines across projects.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Jenkins Shared Library = reusable Groovy code in Git; @Library; DRY pipeline functions

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What does Jenkins 'post' block do in a Declarative Pipeline?
opts: Defines actions to take after all stages complete: always (cleanup), success (notify success), failure (alert), unstable, aborted, changed (status changed from last run)
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Post block runs after pipeline stages. Actions: always (cleanup, close connections), success (tag Docker image, notify success), failure (alert Slack, send email), changed (status changed from last build).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Jenkins post block = actions after pipeline: always/success/failure/unstable/changed

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Jenkins 'agent any' vs 'agent { label }' in a pipeline?
opts: agent any: run on any available agent; agent { label 'docker' }: run only on agents with the 'docker' label; used to target specific capabilities
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: agent any: Jenkins picks any available agent. agent { label 'docker' }: run only on agents labelled 'docker' (have Docker installed). stage-level agents override pipeline-level for specific stages.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: agent any = any available; agent label = specific capability; override per-stage if needed

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Jenkins credentials management?
opts: Jenkins' built-in secure credential store for API keys, passwords, SSH keys, and TLS certs; referenced in pipelines via withCredentials block without exposing values in logs
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Jenkins Credentials: stored securely in Jenkins (not in Jenkinsfile or Git). Reference: withCredentials([string(credentialsId: 'api-key', variable: 'API_KEY')]) { sh 'curl -H auth:$API_KEY ...' }. Never log credentials.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Jenkins Credentials = secure store for secrets; withCredentials{} to use in pipeline without exposure

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Jenkins SCM polling?
opts: Jenkins periodically checking the source code repository for changes and triggering a build if new commits are found; less efficient than webhooks
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: SCM polling: Jenkins checks Git repo at intervals (e.g., H/5 * * * * = every 5 min) for new commits. Less efficient than webhooks (always running checks). Useful when webhooks aren't possible (firewall restrictions).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: SCM polling = Jenkins periodically checks repo for changes; less efficient than webhooks

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Jenkins build artifact?
opts: A file produced by a build (JAR, WAR, Docker image, zip) that can be archived, tested, deployed, or published to a repository as the output of a CI pipeline
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Build artifacts: outputs of a build step (compiled JAR, WAR, zip, Docker image). Archived with archiveArtifacts in Jenkinsfile. Published to Nexus/Artifactory. Docker images pushed to ECR/Docker Hub.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Build artifact = output of build (JAR/WAR/image); archive + publish for deployment

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Nexus/Artifactory in a CI/CD pipeline?
opts: An artifact repository manager that stores and distributes build artifacts (JARs, Docker images, npm packages, etc.) for CI/CD pipelines
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Nexus (Sonatype) and Artifactory (JFrog): binary repository managers. Store: Maven JARs, Docker images, npm packages, Python wheels, Helm charts. Act as proxy caches for public repos and publish internal artifacts.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Nexus/Artifactory = artifact repository manager; stores built JARs/images; CI/CD publish step

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is AWS IAM Policy?
opts: A JSON document defining permissions specifying which AWS actions are allowed or denied on which resources for which principals
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: IAM Policy: JSON document with: Effect (Allow/Deny), Action (s3:GetObject), Resource (arn:aws:s3:::bucket/*), Principal (who). Attach to users, groups, or roles. Evaluate: explicit deny > explicit allow > default deny.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: IAM Policy = JSON document: Effect + Action + Resource; attach to users/groups/roles

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is AWS Secrets Manager?
opts: AWS's credential storage for AWS console
A managed service for securely storing, rotating, and accessing secrets (API keys, DB passwords, OAuth tokens) with automatic rotation and fine-grained IAM access control
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Secrets Manager: store secrets securely, rotate automatically (RDS passwords), integrate with IAM for access control, audit with CloudTrail, retrieve in code via SDK. Alternative: AWS SSM Parameter Store (simpler, cheaper).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Secrets Manager = managed secret store with auto-rotation + IAM control; vs SSM Parameter Store

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is AWS CodeBuild?
opts: AWS's fully managed build service that compiles code, runs tests, and produces deployment artifacts; no servers to manage, scales automatically
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: CodeBuild: serverless build service. Specify buildspec.yml (build commands), choose runtime image. Scales automatically, pay per build minute. Integrates with CodePipeline, GitHub Actions, Jenkins. No build servers to manage.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: CodeBuild = serverless build service; buildspec.yml; scales auto; pay per minute

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is AWS CodeDeploy?
opts: AWS's managed deployment service automating application deployments to EC2, Lambda, and ECS with deployment strategies: blue/green, canary, linear
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: CodeDeploy: managed deployment service. Deployment groups target EC2/ECS/Lambda. Strategies: All-at-once (fast, downtime), Rolling (gradual, minimal downtime), Blue/Green (zero downtime), Canary.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: CodeDeploy = managed deploy to EC2/Lambda/ECS; blue/green + canary strategies

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is AWS Elastic Container Service (ECS)?
opts: AWS's native container orchestration service using Task Definitions and Services; supports EC2 launch type (manage hosts) and Fargate launch type (serverless)
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: ECS: Task Definitions (define container config: image, CPU, memory, ports, env), Services (maintain N tasks running, integrate with ALB). Launch types: EC2 (manage nodes) or Fargate (serverless, no EC2 management).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: ECS = AWS container orchestration; Task Definition + Service; EC2 or Fargate launch type

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is AWS IAM best practice for EC2 instances?
opts: Use access keys stored in the application
Use IAM Roles (Instance Profiles) attached to EC2 instances instead of embedding access keys; roles provide temporary credentials automatically rotated by AWS
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: EC2 IAM Role (Instance Profile): attach a role to EC2, application uses AWS SDK to retrieve temporary credentials automatically. No access keys stored on EC2. Credentials auto-rotate. Best practice for all AWS SDK usage.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: EC2 best practice = IAM Role (Instance Profile); no access keys on EC2; auto-rotating creds

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is AWS Config?
opts: AWS's service that tracks configuration changes to AWS resources over time and evaluates them against desired compliance rules
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: AWS Config: records configuration history of AWS resources. Config Rules: evaluate resources against compliance rules (EC2 must have encrypted EBS, S3 buckets must not be public). Enables continuous compliance monitoring.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: AWS Config = track resource config changes + compliance rules; continuous compliance monitoring

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Kubernetes ResourceQuota?
opts: A K8s object limiting total resource consumption (CPU, memory, pod count, PVC count) within a namespace, preventing one team from monopolising cluster resources
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: ResourceQuota applied to namespace: limits total CPU, memory, pod count, service count, PVC count, configmap count. Prevents resource hogging in multi-tenant clusters. Pair with LimitRange for per-pod defaults.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: ResourceQuota = namespace-level resource limits; prevents monopolising cluster resources

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Kubernetes node drain?
opts: Evicting all pods from a node (moving them to other nodes) before maintenance; respects PodDisruptionBudget; use kubectl drain node-name --ignore-daemonsets
Deleting a node from the cluster
Restarting all pods on a node
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: kubectl drain node: evicts all evictable pods (DaemonSet pods ignored), respects PodDisruptionBudgets (won't remove pods below minAvailable). Marks node as unschedulable (cordon). Use before node maintenance.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: kubectl drain = evict all pods for node maintenance; respects PDB; then cordon node

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Kubernetes node affinity?
opts: Rules controlling which nodes a pod can be scheduled on based on node labels; requiredDuringScheduling (hard requirement) or preferredDuringScheduling (soft preference)
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Node affinity: schedule pods on nodes with specific labels. Required: pod won't schedule if no matching node. Preferred: try to match, but schedule elsewhere if no match. Use for: GPU nodes, specific zones, hardware requirements.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Node affinity = schedule pods on nodes with specific labels; required(hard) or preferred(soft)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Kubernetes ServiceAccount?
opts: A Kubernetes identity for pods (not humans) that provides an authentication identity for processes running in pods to call the K8s API
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: ServiceAccount: K8s identity for pod processes. Pods use ServiceAccount token to authenticate to K8s API. Mount token as volume. Use RBAC (Role + RoleBinding) to grant ServiceAccount specific permissions.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: ServiceAccount = K8s identity for pods; token auth to K8s API; bind to RBAC roles

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Kubernetes Helm values.yaml?
opts: A file defining default configuration values for a Helm Chart that can be overridden during helm install/upgrade with --set or custom values files
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: values.yaml: default values for Helm Chart templates. Override: helm install myapp ./chart --set image.tag=v2.0 or helm install myapp ./chart -f custom-values.yaml. Enables environment-specific deployments from same chart.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Helm values.yaml = default chart values; override with --set or -f for different environments

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Kubernetes webhook?
opts: A callback mechanism where K8s API server calls an external HTTP service for admission control (validating/mutating resources) before persisting them to etcd
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: K8s Admission Webhooks: Mutating (modify resource before storage, e.g., inject sidecar), Validating (reject resources violating policy, e.g., require resource limits). Used by Istio, OPA, Kyverno for policy enforcement.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: K8s Admission Webhook = called by API server before persisting; mutating (modify) + validating (reject)

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Kustomize in Kubernetes?
opts: A Kubernetes-native configuration customisation tool built into kubectl (kubectl apply -k) that patches YAML files for different environments without templates
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Kustomize: overlay-based configuration. Base directory (common resources) + overlay directories (environment-specific patches). No templates (unlike Helm). Built into kubectl. Used for: dev/staging/prod environment variants.
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Kustomize = overlay-based K8s config customisation; base + overlays; built into kubectl

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is a Kubernetes sidecar container?
opts: A secondary container in the same Pod as the main application container, sharing its network and storage to provide supporting functionality (logging, proxy, auth, config sync)
it_infrastructure_devops.expl: Sidecar pattern: secondary container alongside main container in same Pod. Examples: Istio Envoy proxy (service mesh), Fluentd log shipper (ship app logs), Vault agent (inject secrets), git-sync (sync config from Git).
it_infrastructure_devops.takeaway: Sidecar = secondary container in same Pod; shares network+storage; proxy/logging/secrets

it_infrastructure_devops.q: What is Ansible 'with_items' / 'loop' directive?
